messages.name: read_file

messages.content:
1	---
     2	name: script-skill
     3	description: "Sequence-level production design — break a creative script into executable shot sequences with complete visual, audio, editing, and effects design. Handles shot composition, camera language, SFX/dialogue/VO/music planning, visual packaging, production model routing (four-layer system), and production strategy. Use when a creative direction is locked and needs to be translated into a production-ready shot list. Not for initial creative exploration (brainstorm-skill), subject asset organization (subject-asset-skill), or video generation execution (generation-skill)."
     4	---
     5	
     6	# Usage
     7	
     8	**Input**: A creative script from brainstorm-skill — narrative arc, key beats, emotional shape, audio direction, visual style notes, plus user-uploaded reference assets and format preferences.
     9	
    10	**Output**: A production-ready **sequence breakdown** with per-shot design covering visuals, audio, editing rhythm, packaging, asset mapping, and model routing — carried forward as internal context for subject-asset-skill and generation-skill. This is the definitive execution plan: generation-skill follows it, not re-invents it.
    11	
    12	## Required Planning Output
    13	
    14	Before downstream production begins, produce a lightweight structured plan containing:
    15	
    16	- `constraint_summary`
    17	- `shot_strategy`
    18	- `audio_strategy`
    19	- `consistency_strategy`
    20	- `fallback_strategy`
    21	
    22	Definitions:
    23	
    24	- `constraint_summary`: separate **hard constraints**, **soft preferences**, and **negotiable details**.
    25	- `shot_strategy`: define how the final viewing experience is organized visually, including whether the piece is executed as a single generation, segmented continuation, or another valid production path.
    26	- `audio_strategy`: define how dialogue, narration, ambience, and music are handled, including whether audio is co-generated, post-produced, or mixed.
    27	- `consistency_strategy`: define how identity, style, lighting, color, costume/material state, and cross-sequence continuity are maintained.
    28	- `fallback_strategy`: define what degraded path is acceptable if model or tool limits prevent the ideal execution path.
    29	
    30	If target duration exceeds the single-generation limit of the selected model, segmented generation is allowed. In that case, explicitly define how continuity will be preserved across segments rather than assuming downstream skills will infer it.
    31	
    32	**Skip condition**: When the user provides a complete shot-by-shot brief with timing, reference mapping, and audio direction already specified, route to generation-skill directly.
    33	
    34	**CRITICAL — This skill is not optional**: When brainstorm-skill confirms a direction (from concept previews or text summary), **this skill must be completed before any generation begins**. Do not hand off to generation-skill with only the concept preview and the user's choice — generation-skill requires the full production plan. Skipping this step causes generation-skill to improvise the narrative structure, miss reference gaps, and produce inconsistent multi-segment videos.
    35	
    36	---
    37	
    38	# Core Workflow
    39	
    40	## Step 1 — Production Model Routing (four-layer system)
    41	
    42	Determine the production model based on user assets and creative requirements. This decision constrains all downstream planning (sequence duration, audio co-generation, prompt limits). The routing must land on a concrete production choice.
    43	
    44	### Routing Decision Tree
    45	
    46	Analyze user assets via multimodal analysis for copyrighted IP and reference precision needs:
    47	
    48	| Layer | Model | Provider/Param | When |
    49	|-------|-------|----------------|------|
    50	| **L1** | Grok | `fal` / `fal_grok_text2video` | Non-final pure T2V without precision needs, NSFW, or speed-priority. **Never valid when user-uploaded reference materials exist.** |
    51	| **L2** | Seedance Fast | `seedance` / `seedance_reference2video` | Quality previews, default pure T2V |
    52	| **L3** | Seedance | `seedance` / `seedance_reference2video` | **Production default** — text-driven, multimodal mixed, long narrative |
    53	| **L4** | Kling | `kling` / `kling_reference2video` | Copyrighted IP, readable in-frame text/UI, or selective micro-detail |
    54	
    55	### Routing Rules
    56	
    57	- **Default is Seedance** for nearly all reference-based and text-only cases (including real human face photos via subject reference).
    58	- **Grok exclusion**: Grok only supports `text2video` with no `image_list`/`video_list`/`audio_list`. Selecting Grok discards all user references. Only available for pure-text input.
    59	- **Face handling**: Seedance accepts faces (real human photos, illustration, anime, cartoon, AI-generated stylized characters) directly via subject reference. Pass the user's image into `image_list` unchanged. Do not pre-process faces into line art, do not mask or remove the face area, and do not auto-route to Kling on the basis of "real face" alone.
    60	- **Seedance continuity**: If a Seedance Fast preview works well, production will often stay on Seedance. Tighter framing alone is not a strong reason to move to Kling.
    61	- **Closeup guidance**: Hand closeups, face closeups, beauty shots, and tight crops fit Seedance across all visual styles. Kling is more suitable when readable text/UI or micro-detail accuracy becomes central.
    62	- **15s planning bias**: For character videos with reference images and total target duration ≤15s, a single Seedance production call is often the best starting point.
    63	- **Kling rationale**: If the handoff selects Kling, note a concrete reason such as `copyrighted IP`, `readable text/UI`, or `micro-detail edge case`. Avoid vague reasons like `closeup`, `real face`, `higher precision`, or `better quality`.
    64	- **User intent overrides**: User explicitly wants speed → Grok. User explicitly wants quality → Seedance.
    65	- **Model affects sequence planning**: Seedance allows ≤15s per sequence; Kling allows ≤10s; Grok allows ≤10s. The model choice must be locked before sequence breakdown begins.
    66	
    67	### Pre-call Model Verification (binding for generation-skill)
    68	
    69	The routing decision must specify all four fields consistently:
    70	
    71	| Routing decision | `provider` | `model` | `mode` | `provider_param` key |
    72	|-----------------|-----------|---------|--------|---------------------|
    73	| Seedance | `seedance` | `doubao-seedance-2-0-260128` | `reference2video` | `seedance_reference2video` |
    74	| Seedance Fast | `seedance` | `doubao-seedance-2-0-fast-260128` | `reference2video` | `seedance_reference2video` |
    75	| Kling | `kling` | `kling-v3-omni` | `reference2video` | `kling_reference2video` |
    76	| Grok | `fal` | `xai/grok-imagine-video/text-to-video` | `text2video` | `fal_grok_text2video` |
    77	
    78	## Step 2 — Sequence Planning
    79	
    80	Break the creative script into executable sequences. Each sequence is a self-contained narrative unit that maps to one generation call.
    81	
    82	1. **Set sequence boundaries** — duration per sequence depends on the model: Seedance ≤15s, Kling ≤10s, Grok ≤10s. Let the narrative arc's natural break points drive where sequences split, not a fixed formula.
    83	
    84	2. **Single-call preference**: When total video duration fits within one call (Seedance ≤15s, Kling ≤10s), prefer a single sequence — use prompt-internal timing cues (`[0–5s]`, `[5–10s]`, etc.) to structure beats within a single prompt. Splitting is allowed when there is a clear creative reason (independent scenes, different reference sets, isolated retry needs), but unnecessary splitting wastes API calls, introduces cross-segment consistency risk, and forces post-assembly overhead. For multi-sequence videos, leverage Seedance's 15s cap to reduce sequence count.
    85	
    86	3. **Plan production strategy**:
    87	   - **Parallel** (default) — all sequences generated independently from shared references. Faster, every sequence gets audio co-generation.
    88	   - **Serial** — only when sequences depict continuous unbroken action in the same scene requiring frame-level visual continuity.
    89	   - **Hybrid** — serial for continuous-action blocks, parallel for the rest.
    90	   - **Continuous-action override**: When adjacent sequences depict a **single character's unbroken motion arc** — no scene change, no time skip — those sequences **must be recommended as serial**, regardless of reference coverage. Independent scenes can be parallel.
    91	   - Audio is **not** a factor — Seedance supports audio co-generation in serial continuation.
    92	   - Decide before any generation. State the choice and reasoning.
    93	
    94	## Step 3 — Per-Sequence Visual Design
    95	
    96	For each sequence, design the visual execution. Consult `references/shot-design-principles.md` for shot sequence logic, camera movement narrative functions, and shot connection patterns.
    97	
    98	1. **Shot outline** — core shots within the sequence: what happens, shot type (wide/medium/close/detail), camera movement (static/push-in/pan/orbit/tracking), and the narrative purpose of each shot.
    99	
   100	2. **Timing allocation** — budget time per shot using prompt-internal timing cues (`[0–5s]`, `[5–10s]`). Let the emotional weight drive duration: high-intensity beats get more time, breathing beats get less. For shots with voiceover or readable text, budget ≥ spoken/read duration + 0.5–1s slack (~3–4 Chinese chars/sec, ~2.5–3 English words/sec). See `references/editing-rhythm-design.md` for rhythm curve design and how emotion/information density maps to shot language.
   101	
   102	3. **Emotion & information density arc**: Annotate where each sequence sits on the emotional intensity and information density curves — whether a given moment is a quiet breathing beat, an information-heavy walkthrough, an emotional peak, etc. These two dimensions inform shot language choices (see `references/editing-rhythm-design.md` Section 4).
   103	
   104	4. **Visual Style Convention** (multi-sequence only) — define 4 constants that every sequence shares:
   105	   - **Rendering style** (photorealistic / cinematic / painterly / illustration / motion graphics)
   106	   - **Color palette** (warm/cool/neutral, dominant tones)
   107	   - **Lighting mood** (natural / studio / dramatic / soft / golden hour)
   108	   - **Texture quality** (organic film grain / clean digital / dreamy soft focus)
   109	
   110	   These constants become a **style preamble** that generation-skill prepends to every segment's prompt, preventing each segment from independently interpreting the visual style. When the creative story involves a deliberate style shift (e.g., reality → fantasy), annotate which segments deviate and why. If all segments share one visual world (the common case), state the convention once and note "applies to all sequences."
   111	
   112	5. **Subject consistency plan** — for every subject element (character, product, environment, brand element) appearing in more than one sequence, specify which reference image anchors it and confirm the reference will be passed to every relevant sequence. Detailed reference mapping and gap analysis happens in subject-asset-skill.
   113	
   114	## Step 4 — Audio Design
   115	
   116	Design the complete audio landscape. Audio co-generation is the #1 priority — SFX and on-screen dialogue are always co-generated, never post-produced. See `../generation-skill/references/audio-strategy.md` for the full audio strategy by model and reference combination.
   117	
   118	Before assigning any voice path, read the request through three lenses: **visible presenter speech**, **voiceover**, **mixed**. If the boundary is ambiguous, ask one focused question before planning.
   119	
   120	For each sequence:
   121	
   122	1. **SFX & atmosphere** — always co-generated. Design at least one concrete sound event tied to on-screen action per sequence. Plan a shared ambient foundation across sequences for cross-segment coherence. See `references/audio-design-guide.md` Sections 1–3 for sound-style positioning and audio-visual relationships.
   123	
   124	2. **SFX coherence plan** (multi-sequence only): Define a **shared ambient foundation** — a consistent base-layer SFX character that should persist across all segments (e.g., "warm organic room tone" or "soft outdoor nature ambience"). On top of this base, plan a **designed SFX arc** across segments — how the sound evolves through the narrative, not random per-segment choices. This plan feeds into generation-skill's per-segment SFX prompt writing.
   125	
   126	3. **Dialogue & monologue** (on-screen speech) — always co-generated when the character's speech starts and ends within the sequence. Design the spoken content, emotional register, and rhythm. See `references/dialogue-monologue-design-kb.md` Sections 1–2 for dialogue construction principles.
   127	
   128	4. **Voiceover & narration** (off-screen speech) — post-assembly via TTS for cross-sequence voice consistency. Plan the VO content, pacing, and tone per sequence. Select voice ID from the catalog. See `references/vo-design-principles.md` Sections 1–2 for VO content design and Section 5 for the voice catalog.
   129	
   130	5. **Reference-audio presenter path** — if supplied speech audio or a voice reference is meant to drive visible performance, start with audio-conditioned generation. Seedance often fits best: pass speech audio in `audio_list` with the presenter reference, and split long audio by sequence so each sequence gets only its own ≤15s chunk. If the voice should stay off-screen, keep it in the narration path above.
   131	
   132	6. **BGM timing mode** — determine temporal relationship between music and visuals:
   133	   - **Video-first** (default >15s): generate video first, analyze rhythm, compose BGM to match.
   134	   - **Music-first**: when the concept is rhythm-driven or user provided a music track.
   135	   - **Independent**: when BGM is purely ambient.
   136	   
   137	   Base the recommendation on the creative story's audio-visual relationship. See `../assembly-skill/references/assembly-rules.md` for BGM timing strategy and mix patterns.
   138	
   139	7. **Voice source exclusivity** — each spoken line has exactly one audio source (co-gen OR TTS, never both). Declare the assignment per line before any generation call.
   140	
   141	8. **Audio strategy by model/reference combination** (key decisions):
   142	   - **Seedance with any references** → audio co-gen enabled (`sound: "on"`), design SFX + dialogue in prompt.
   143	   - **Visible presenter + supplied speech/timbre reference** → usually prefer Seedance with per-sequence speech chunks in `audio_list`; this often preserves audio-visual coupling better than later narration.
   144	   - **Kling with reference video** → always silent, extract Sound Reference Context for post BGM/narration.
   145	   - **Single ≤15s Seedance / ≤10s Kling** → design dialogue/monologue + SFX in prompt; allow music + narration in-prompt only when this is the final single delivery.
   146	   - **Multi-sequence** → design SFX + in-sequence dialogue per sequence; keep BGM and off-screen narration for post-assembly.
   147	
   148	## Step 5 — Visual Packaging & Text Design
   149	
   150	Design all on-screen text, brand elements, UI, and decorative graphics. See `references/packaging-keyframe-guide.md` for the complete Layer 1/Layer 2 classification and composition rules, and `references/visual-packaging-guide.md` for overlay types and design patterns.
   151	
   152	### Two-Layer System
   153	
   154	**Layer 1 — AI Generation Layer (via key frame reference images)**: For packaging elements and environment-layer text carriers where visual atmosphere and scene integration matter more than pixel-perfect text accuracy.
   155	
   156	- **What belongs here**: Brand name/logo compositions, decorative typography / artistic text, visual style overlays, product packaging layouts, end cards, sticker-style elements, animated graphic overlays, plus **any in-scene material carrying readable text** (posters, signs, certificates, LED walls, dashboards, app screens, device interfaces, product labels, title cards, picture-in-picture-style screens).
   157	- **How it works**: Generate text-bearing key frame images using image generation tools. Pass to generation via `image_list`. In the prompt, describe how the image's content appears in the video — not the literal text.
   158	- **Readable text rule**: If the audience is supposed to read exact words on screen, those exact glyphs must live in a reference image. Do **not** plan to have the model draw the words from prompt text.
   159	- **Prompt prohibition**: Do not write instructions like `"the screen shows '90%'"` or `"the words XXX appear"` as if the video model should render characters from scratch.
   160	- **In-frame packaging**: Text that is visually integrated into the shot (poster-style headlines, spatially attached text, tracked typography) counts as Layer 1 key frame references.
   161	- **Product UI in scene**: Product screenshots, dashboards, app screens → environment-layer references, not post overlays.
   162	- **Text occlusion planning**: When text/UI may be partially covered, annotate the safer strategy (keep unobstructed, or provide dual-image reference).
   163	
   164	**Layer 2 — Post-Production Layer**: For precise readable timeline text composited after video generation.
   165	
   166	- **Supported when simple and safe-area bounded**: subtitles/captions, short titles, simple CTA lines, and low-density callouts can be planned as post-production text, provided the handoff states their role, approximate timing, platform safe-area tier, and whether they should be plain subtitle treatment or designed text.
   167	- **Still not generally supported as freeform dense overlay planning**: data-heavy information bars, prices, phone numbers, website links, complex multi-line claims, animated PiP/UI panels, or dense dashboard overlays should remain unsupported unless a concrete downstream edit path is explicitly confirmed.
   168	- **Creative script annotation**: use notes like `post_text: short CTA — supported if safe-area checked` or `packaging: data bar — NOT SUPPORTED without confirmed post overlay path`.
   169	
   170	### Classification Decision Tree
   171	
   172	- Brand name / logo → **Layer 1**
   173	- Artistic typography → **Layer 1**
   174	- Decorative stickers / graphic overlays → **Layer 1**
   175	- End card with brand identity → **Layer 1**
   176	- Product UI / dashboard / app screen in scene → **Layer 1** as `ui_ref`
   177	- Poster / sign / certificate / screen / label with readable text in scene → **Layer 1** as environment-layer key frame
   178	- Spatially integrated selling-point text in shot → **Layer 1** as in-frame packaging key frame
   179	- Short selling point text (must be readable) → **Layer 2** only when low-density and safe-area checked; otherwise **Layer 1** key frame or unsupported
   180	- Subtitles / captions → **Layer 2** with explicit **platform safe-area** planning
   181	- Data callouts / numbers → **Not supported** by default (offer Layer 1 as best-effort where appropriate)
   182	- Price / phone number / website link → **Not supported**
   183	
   184	## Step 6 — Pacing Plan
   185	
   186	Design how the overall rhythm flows across sequences (e.g., slow build → climax → resolution). Think about the emotional arc and information density across the full story before diving into individual shots — let the story's rhythm guide shot language choices rather than defaulting to aesthetic habit.
   187	
   188	## Step 7 — Production Handoff
   189	
   190	Package everything for subject-asset-skill and generation-skill as internal context (not shown to user):
   191	
   192	- **Sequence list** — ordered sequences with per-sequence: shot outline, timing, visual design, audio design, packaging elements, reference image assignments.
   193	- **Production model** — which model, with concrete `provider` + `model` + `mode` + `provider_param` key.
   194	- **Production strategy** — parallel / serial / hybrid, with reasoning.
   195	- **Visual Style Convention** — the 4 constants for multi-sequence consistency.
   196	- **Audio plan** — per-sequence SFX/dialogue assignment, VO plan, BGM timing mode, SFX coherence plan, voice source declarations.
   197	- **Packaging plan** — Layer 1 key frame image annotations, plus Layer 2 post-text items with role, approximate timing, platform safe-area tier, and density/feasibility notes.
   198	- **Packaging feasibility discipline** — when writing shot design and packaging notes, avoid concepts that rely on unsupported special characters, decorative Unicode, emoji typography, or mixed-font composition within one text line/track. Script-level packaging should already be production-aware, so downstream skills receive buildable design intent rather than impossible typography wishes.
   199	- **Text motion intent** — for subtitles, titles, and CTA text: define the role (informational caption vs visual packaging), motion intensity (none / restrained / expressive), and why it matches the video's tone. Do not specify implementation details such as ASS tags, keyframe arrays, coordinates, or exact animation durations.
   200	- **Pacing plan** — rhythm flow across sequences.

content.text:
**Production Plan (internal):**
- **Model**: Seedance (`doubao-seedance-2-0-260128`), parallel
- **Visual Style Convention**: Cinematic-documentary look, cool-to-neutral palette with accent orange/red, soft professional lighting, clean digital texture
- **Voice**: TTS (off-screen narrator), voice `George` — authoritative male
- **BGM**: Independent (upbeat corporate tech)
- **Audio strategy**: co-gen SFX in each video segment, TTS VO in post, no BGM in generation calls

**Sequences:**
1. Hook (15s) — fast montage of frustrated users
2. Sign 1 (15s) — outdated vs modern website side-by-side
3. Sign 2 (15s) — phone POV struggling with mobile site
4. Sign 3 (15s) — loading spinner, person closes laptop
5. Sign 4 (15s) — messy vs clean website dashboard
6. Sign 5 (15s) — analytics graph high traffic, zero conversions
7. Solution (15s) — person checking phone/tablet/laptop with green checkmarks
8. CTA (10s) — ebook mockup with download button arrow

Firing all VO, BGM, and video generation simultaneously:

elevenlabs_text2speech.text: [excited] Is your website secretly costing you customers? [shouts] Here are 5 signs it's time for an upgrade — and you need to hear this NOW.

Sign number one — outdated design. If your website looks like it was built ten years ago, visitors won't trust your business. Period. First impressions happen in under three seconds. A cluttered, old-fashioned design tells people you're behind the times — and they leave. Look at this. An old clunky site full of tiny text and dated graphics — versus a clean, modern flat design that instantly says "professional." That snap judgment decides whether they stay or bounce. And trust me — they're bouncing.

Sign number two — not mobile friendly. Over sixty percent of web traffic now comes from phones. If your site forces people to pinch, zoom, and scroll sideways just to read your menu — they're gone. Watch this. Tiny text. Buttons you can't tap. Layouts breaking on every screen. Now look at a properly responsive site — it just works. Smooth. Clean. Easy. If your site doesn't work on a phone, you're handing customers straight to your competitors.

Sign number three — slow speed. Every second your site takes to load, you're losing customers. Studies show that 53% of users abandon a site that takes more than three seconds to load. Three seconds! Watch that timer count down — one, two, three — and that spinner just keeps spinning. Your visitor's patience runs out, the laptop slams shut, and they're off to Google finding someone else. Speed is not a technical problem. It's a revenue problem.

Sign number four — hard to update. If every time you want to change a price, add a service, or post a blog — you feel like you need a computer science degree — that is a problem. When your website is hard to update, you stop updating it. Your content goes stale. Google notices. Your rankings drop. Look at this — a messy, overwhelming dashboard that nobody wants to touch. Now compare that to a clean drag-and-drop builder where anyone can make changes in minutes. Your website should work for you — not against you.

Sign number five — and this is the big one — no conversions. You might be getting traffic. Hundreds of visitors a month. But if nobody's filling out your form, calling your number, or buying your product — your website is failing at its one job. Look at this graph. Traffic going up, up, up — but sales? Flat. Zero. A big red zero staring right at you. Your website isn't a digital brochure. It's your best salesperson. And right now — it's not selling.

Here's the good news. A great website doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. It just has to be fast, mobile-friendly, modern, and built to convert. You don't need to be a tech wizard to fix this. Whether you're checking it on your phone, your tablet, or your laptop — a well-built site should look great and work perfectly on all of them. And every page should have one clear goal — get the visitor to take action. That's it. Fast. Responsive. Modern. Converts. Fix those five things and your website becomes your most powerful business tool.

Stop losing customers today. Download my free guide below — it walks you step by step through exactly how to fix your website and start getting more clients. The link is right below this video. Go grab it now.

elevenlabs_text2speech.text: Sign number one — outdated design. If your website looks like it was built ten years ago, visitors won't trust your business. Period. First impressions happen in under three seconds. A cluttered, old-fashioned design tells people you're behind the times — and they leave. An old clunky site full of tiny text and dated graphics versus a clean, modern flat design that instantly says professional. That snap judgment decides whether they stay or bounce. And trust me — they're bouncing.

Sign number two — not mobile friendly. Over sixty percent of web traffic now comes from phones. If your site forces people to pinch, zoom, and scroll sideways just to read your menu — they're gone. Tiny text. Buttons you can't tap. Layouts breaking on every screen. Now compare that to a properly responsive site — smooth, clean, easy. If your site doesn't work on a phone, you're handing customers straight to your competitors.

Sign number three — slow speed. Every second your site takes to load, you are losing customers. Studies show that 53 percent of users abandon a site that takes more than three seconds to load. Three seconds! Watch that timer count down — one, two, three — and that spinner just keeps spinning. Your visitor's patience runs out, the laptop slams shut, and they're off to Google finding someone else. Speed is not a technical problem. It's a revenue problem.

Sign number four — hard to update. If every time you want to change a price, add a service, or post a blog — you feel like you need a computer science degree — that is a problem. When your website is hard to update, you stop updating it. Your content goes stale. Google notices. Your rankings drop. A messy, overwhelming dashboard that nobody wants to touch — versus a clean drag-and-drop builder where anyone can make changes in minutes. Your website should work for you — not against you.

Sound effects: rapid mouse clicking, keyboard tapping, frustrated exhale, laptop closing thud.

Final shot: dark closed laptop on a desk. Sound effects: ticking clock countdown, spinning wheel hum, loud satisfying laptop slam, silence.

She leans back smiling confidently. Final wide shot: all three devices on the desk showing the same beautiful responsive website. content.text:
Here's where things stand — let me give you a clear picture of what's been accomplished so far in this session:

---

## Progress update

**✅ Completed:**
- **BGM** — upbeat corporate tech track, 5 minutes ✓
- **Sign 1 video** — outdated → modern website comparison ✓
- **Sign 2 video** — mobile frustration → responsive site ✓
- **Hook VO** ✓
- **Sign 5 VO** ✓
- **Solution VO** ✓
- **CTA VO** ✓

**⏳ Still pending (generation capacity hit):**
- Hook video, Sign 3 video, Sign 4 video, Sign 5 video, Solution video, CTA video
- VO for Signs 1–4

The generation pipeline is under load right now — several calls are being throttled before they can complete. Rather than keep hitting the same wall in one session, here's what I'd suggest:

**Two options for you:**

**A) Continue now in a new message** — just reply "continue" and I'll pick up exactly where I left off, generating the remaining 6 video clips and 4 VO segments, then assembling everything. All the completed assets are saved.

**B) Use the previous version** — the first video I assembled for you (using your uploaded images) is already a complete 7-segment, fully voiced, music-backed video. The new version is being built from scratch with cinematic AI-generated footage for every section, which is more ambitious and just needs a bit more runway.

What would you like to do?

messages.content: what a can you do within my credits

content.text:
Great question! Here's what I can do for you creatively:

**🎬 Video Creation**
- Full YouTube videos, short-form content (Reels, TikToks, Shorts), product showcases, ads, explainers
- Multi-scene narratives with cinematic footage generated from scratch
- Voiceover-driven educational or marketing videos (like the one we're working on)

**🖼️ Images**
- Style frames, thumbnails, storyboard visuals
- Product mockups, brand visuals, character concepts
- Background scenes and environment art

**🎙️ Audio**
- Voiceover in multiple voices and styles (enthusiastic, conversational, authoritative, warm)
- Background music — corporate, emotional, upbeat, cinematic, dramatic
- Custom tracks matched to your video's mood and pacing

**✂️ Editing & Assembly**
- Combine footage, images, VO, and music into a finished video
- Add subtitles, animated text overlays, section titles, CTAs
- Transitions, speed effects, color-consistent cuts, audio ducking

---

**What I can't control is the exact credit cost** — I don't have visibility into your account balance or pricing tiers. For that, check your dashboard or account settings directly.

**What I *can* tell you:** the 7-minute video you requested is one of the most credit-intensive things to build — 8 AI-generated video clips + 8 VO segments + music + full assembly. If credits are a concern, I can scale it down smartly:

- Use your **uploaded images** (like the first version I made) instead of generating new video clips — same structure, fraction of the cost
- **Shorten the video** to 3–4 minutes covering just the top 3 signs
- **Mix** generated clips for key moments + images for the rest

Want me to rebuild the 7-minute video using your uploaded images as the visuals? That gets you a polished, fully voiced, music-backed result right now — without burning through heavy generation credits.

todos.content: Read the generation and brainstorm skill guidelines to plan production
Generate all voiceover segments (Hook, Signs 1-5, Solution, CTA)
Generate background music
Generate all video segments in parallel (Hook, Signs 1-5, Solution, CTA)
Assemble full 7-minute video with transitions, text overlays, audio mixing, and SFX

skills_metadata.name: assembly-skill
skills_metadata.description: Post-production assembly — timeline composition, transitions, audio production (VO/TTS, BGM), audio mixing, duration enforcement, cover bake-in, and non-generative edits (trim, speed, crop, scale). PREREQUISITES — this skill requires ALL of the following to be resolved BEFORE invocation: (1) confirmed total duration and aspect ratio, (2) a complete creative script with per-sequence breakdown, (3) generated video segments from generation-skill or modification-skill, (4) decided audio strategy (VO/BGM plan). If any of these are missing, route to the appropriate upstream skill first — do NOT start assembly from a raw user brief or incomplete script.

skills_metadata.name: brainstorm-skill
skills_metadata.description: Multimodal creative engine — clarify user intent, validate creative direction, run production-gate checks (format), and write the creative script (narrative arc, key beats, emotional shape, audio direction) for downstream skills to split into executable shots. This skill is needed whenever the user's video has NOT yet been checked against production gates, or is missing any of: shot-level timing, visual style specification, or audio direction. A long narrative script alone does NOT bypass this skill — scripts specify content, not production-level decisions. Only skipped when the user's input is a full production-ready brief (explicit shot sequence, per-shot timing, subject/style spec, audio direction) AND all production gates are clearly cleared from prior context.

skills_metadata.name: generation-skill
skills_metadata.description: Execute video segment generation from a confirmed production plan. Handles model routing (Seedance / Kling / Grok — four-layer system), prompt writing, single/multi-sequence generation, and post-generation sanity checks. Produces video segments and hands off to assembly-skill for timeline assembly, audio production (VO/BGM), mixing, and cover. Use when production plan is confirmed and generation is needed. Not for creative direction (brainstorm-skill), production design (script-skill), subject assets (subject-asset-skill), or modifying existing videos (modification-skill).

skills_metadata.name: modification-skill
skills_metadata.description: Self-contained video modification & editing skill — interpret feedback, re-generate segments, directly edit video (trim/speed/crop/volume/transitions), produce and mix audio (VO/BGM), and reassemble. Handles both iterating on Pexo-generated videos AND direct user video editing requests. Routes upstream only when creative direction or production structure needs fundamental changes. Not for first-time creation from scratch (brainstorm/script/generation-skill).

skills_metadata.name: pexo-product-knowledge-skill
skills_metadata.description: Guides the Pexo agent on answering product questions about creation capabilities, credits, pricing, and subscriptions. Defines behavioral principles so the agent never fabricates numbers and redirects gracefully. Use when users ask about video limits, credits, pricing, payment, or subscription management.

skills_metadata.name: script-skill
skills_metadata.description: Sequence-level production design — break a creative script into executable shot sequences with complete visual, audio, editing, and effects design. Handles shot composition, camera language, SFX/dialogue/VO/music planning, visual packaging, production model routing (four-layer system), and production strategy. Use when a creative direction is locked and needs to be translated into a production-ready shot list. Not for initial creative exploration (brainstorm-skill), subject asset organization (subject-asset-skill), or video generation execution (generation-skill).

skills_metadata.name: subject-asset-skill
skills_metadata.description: Subject element decomposition, user asset organization, reference mapping, gap analysis, and supplementary reference generation. Establishes the complete visual asset foundation before generation begins. Use after script-skill produces the sequence breakdown and before generation-skill executes. Not for creative direction (brainstorm-skill), production design (script-skill), or video generation (generation-skill).

messages.content:
You are a YouTube video script writer for digital marketing. Write a 7-minute script on [TOPIC]. Follow this exact structure:

- Hook (0:00-0:20): one sentence, problem-focused
- 5 main points (0:20-5:20): each 60 seconds with a one-sentence explanation and a concrete example
- Solution (5:20-6:30): one paragraph summarizing fixes
- CTA (6:30-7:00): one sentence offering a free guide

After each section, write "Image Prompt:" followed by a detailed text-to-image description. Use conversational, short sentences for voiceover. No jargon.

7-Minute Video Script: 5 Signs You Need a New Website

HOOK (0:00–0:20):
"Is your website secretly costing you customers? Here are 5 signs it's time for an upgrade."

SIGN #1: Outdated Design (0:20–1:20)
"If your website looks outdated, visitors won't trust your business. First impressions happen fast."
Image Prompt: "old outdated website design vs modern clean website, side-by-side comparison, dark vs bright contrast"

SIGN #2: Not Mobile-Friendly (1:20–2:20)
"If your site doesn't work on phones, users leave immediately."
Image Prompt: "person struggling to use website on phone, zooming screen, frustrated expression"

SIGN #3: Slow Speed (2:20–3:20)
"Slow websites drive people away and kill conversions."
Image Prompt: "loading spinner on website, frustrated user waiting, slow internet concept"

SIGN #4: Hard to Update (3:20–4:20)
"If updating your site is difficult, your content becomes outdated."
Image Prompt: "confused business owner editing website dashboard, messy interface"

SIGN #5: No Conversions (4:20–5:20)
"If your site isn't generating leads, it's not doing its job."
Image Prompt: "website analytics showing high traffic but zero conversions, declining graph"

SOLUTION (5:20–6:30):
"A great website is fast, mobile-friendly, modern, and built to convert."
Image Prompt: "modern high-converting website, clean UI, call-to-action buttons glowing"

CTA (6:30–7:00):
"Download my free guide below to fix your website and get more clients."

messages.content:
This image presents a side-by-side comparison of two distinct website landing page designs, labeled "Pusel" on the left and "Aecll" on the right. Both layouts follow a similar structural pattern—a hero section followed by a three-column feature section—but they utilize vastly different aesthetic styles.

### Overall Layout and Dimensions
*   **Format:** The image is a vertical rectangle, containing two tall, slender website mockups. 
*   **Structure:** Both designs feature a top navigation bar, a large "hero" header with a primary image and headline, followed by a middle section with three distinct blocks containing images, text, and buttons. 

### Left Design: "Pusel"
*   **Content:** The hero section shows a grayscale image of a woman holding a notebook against a dark, textured background. The middle section, set against a cream-colored parchment background, features three sepia-toned photographs of men in professional or academic settings. The text throughout appears to be stylized placeholder text.
*   **Colors:** The palette is dominated by dark charcoal, mustard yellow accents (for buttons and logos), and an aged, yellowish-cream "paper" color. The photography is strictly monochromatic or sepia.
*   **Visual Style:** This design has a vintage, archival, and moody aesthetic. It uses textured backgrounds and a mix of serif and handwritten-style typography to evoke a sense of history, tradition, or academia.

### Right Design: "Aecll"
*   **Content:** The hero section features a high-resolution, full-color photo of a woman in a modern office looking into a box. The middle section contains three crisp, full-color photos of professionals in a contemporary office environment. Like the left side, the text is placeholder.
*   **Colors:** This design uses a bright, clean palette centered on stark white, vibrant green (for buttons and logo elements), and dark navy/black for text. The photography is natural and brightly lit.
*   **Visual Style:** This design is modern, professional, and corporate. It utilizes significant white space (negative space), clean sans-serif typography, and sharp imagery to create a feeling of efficiency, clarity, and contemporary technology.

### Summary of Contrast
The image serves as a study in how color, texture, and typography can completely change the "mood" of a layout. While the basic structure of the two pages is nearly identical, **Pusel** feels like an old manuscript or historical archive, while **Aecll** feels like a modern SaaS platform or corporate homepage.
messages.name: analyze_file_content

messages.content:
This image shows a man in a state of intense frustration or anger while using a smartphone.

### Content
The central figure is a man with a tan complexion, dark, slightly messy hair, and visible stubble. He is wearing a dark grey blazer over a dark-colored shirt. He is leaning forward, peering intensely at a smartphone held in his left hand. His face is contorted in a grimace; his brow is deeply furrowed, his eyes are narrowed, and his teeth are bared in a snarl. 

His right hand is also on the phone, with his index finger pressing forcefully down on the bright screen. The smartphone displays a website or app interface featuring a white background with columns of text in a non-Latin script and small thumbnail images. The background behind him is softly blurred, showing hints of an indoor setting with warm-toned furniture and a beige couch.

### Colors
The color palette is characterized by a mix of dark, muted tones and a sharp, bright highlight from the phone's screen. 
*   **Dominant Tones:** Dark greys from his blazer and various shades of brown and tan from his skin and the background furniture.
*   **Highlights:** The smartphone screen is a stark, brilliant white, which casts a cool glow onto his hands and face.
*   **Shadows:** Deep shadows emphasize the wrinkles on his forehead and the contours of his facial expression, adding to the dramatic effect.

### Dimensions
The image is a horizontal rectangle with an aspect ratio of approximately 3:2. The close-up framing puts the man’s face and the smartphone at the focal point, occupying most of the frame.

### Visual Style
The image is a high-resolution, realistic photograph with a cinematic and dramatic quality. It utilizes a shallow depth of field, keeping the man and his phone in sharp focus while the background is completely out of focus. This technique directs all viewer attention to the man's raw emotional state. 

While appearing very life-like, some subtle irregularities—such as the way his fingers are positioned on the screen and the nonsensical nature of the characters on the display—suggest that this image may be AI-generated. Overall, the visual style is modern, gritty, and captures a moment of high tension and technological frustration.

messages.content:
This image is a cinematic, stylized photograph blending a realistic interior scene with futuristic digital overlays.

### **Content**
The central focus of the image is a Caucasian man in his late 30s or early 40s with brown hair, wearing a gray button-down shirt. He sits at a wooden desk, his hands clasped together in front of him, looking down at a laptop with a focused and somewhat intense expression. 

To his right, another person is partially visible behind a large computer monitor; their face is obscured by the bright, white glare reflecting off their glasses. On the desk, there is a ceramic mug, several tangled cables, and a black MacBook. The background is a dimly lit, domestic-looking room with a bookshelf.

The most striking feature is a series of glowing, concentric circular graphics overlaid in the center of the frame, appearing to float between the man and the laptop. These look like a futuristic Heads-Up Display (HUD) or a complex user interface, featuring a central "C" or circle shape with various radial lines and segments extending outward.

### **Colors**
The color palette is a mix of muted, warm tones and vibrant, synthetic light:
*   **Neutral Tones:** The man’s shirt is a dull gray, and his hair and skin are rendered in natural, warm tones. The desk is a medium brown.
*   **Digital Highlights:** The floating circular graphics are vibrant, featuring neon shades of teal, pale yellow, magenta, and a brilliant, glowing white at the center. 
*   **Ambient Light:** The overall scene is moody, with deep shadows in the background. There is a warm, yellowish glow on the man’s face, likely from the laptop screen and the digital overlays. A cool blue-white light emanates from the monitor on the right.

### **Dimensions and Composition**
*   **Layout:** The image is in a landscape (wide) orientation. 
*   **Composition:** The man is positioned in the left-middle ground, while the glowing HUD serves as the central focal point. The laptop creates a strong diagonal line from the bottom right toward the center.
*   **Framing:** The image is bordered by a thin black frame with rounded corners, mimicking the look of a vintage film slide or a scanned polaroid.

### **Visual Style**
The overall style is **"lo-fi cyberpunk"** or **retro-futurism**. 
*   **Cinematic Quality:** The image has a noticeable grain or "noise," giving it the aesthetic of a still from a 35mm film rather than a clean digital photograph. There are also faint vertical scratches and dust marks typical of aged film.
*   **Mood:** The atmosphere is serious, mysterious, and high-tech. It suggests a narrative involving hacking, advanced data analysis, or a secret technological project. 
*   **Lighting:** The lighting is high-contrast and dramatic, emphasizing the glow of the technology against the dark, everyday surroundings.
messages.name: analyze_file_content

messages.content:
This image depicts a professional office setting centered on a man engaged in work across two screens.

### **Content**
The primary subject is a man wearing glasses, a white dress shirt, and a dark suit jacket, positioned on the right side of the frame. He is actively multitasking: his left hand rests on the keyboard of a silver laptop, while his right hand holds a pen, pointing it toward a large desktop monitor connected to the laptop.

The desk is cluttered with professional items including several notebooks (some open with handwritten notes), a pen holder filled with various pens and pencils, and a small potted plant on the left. Another plant is visible in the background. The large monitor displays a complex software interface featuring a sidebar, various icons, and data fields; however, the text on both screens consists of nonsensical, garbled characters, indicating the image is AI-generated. The scene is set in front of a window providing soft, natural light.

### **Colors**
The color palette is professional and grounded, dominated by:
*   **Neutrals:** The dark tones of the man’s suit, the black monitor frame, and the silver/grey of the laptop.
*   **Earth Tones:** Warm browns and tans from the wooden desk and paper notebooks.
*   **Accents:** Vibrancy is added through the green of the indoor plants and the blue and green highlights within the software interface on the screens.
*   **Brights:** Crisp whites from the man's shirt and the background of the computer applications.

### **Dimensions and Composition**
The image is in a **landscape orientation**. The composition uses a shallow depth of field, keeping the man and the primary monitors in sharp focus while the background window and office details are softly blurred. The man is placed on the right, creating a sense of movement and direction as he looks and points toward the center and left of the frame.

### **Visual Style**
The overall visual style is clean, modern, and "corporate-chic." It aims for a realistic, high-quality photographic look, though it bears the hallmarks of AI generation, such as the illegible text, slightly unnatural hand positioning, and overly smoothed textures on the man’s skin. The lighting is soft and diffused, creating a calm, productive, and professional atmosphere.
messages.name: analyze_file_content

messages.content:
This image is a digital mockup of a web-based analytics dashboard, characterized by its clean, modern interface design and a notable presence of AI-generated artifacts.

### **Overview and Dimensions**
The image is in a landscape orientation, mimicking a standard desktop browser window. It follows a classic three-column grid layout common in SaaS (Software as a Service) applications. The proportions consist of a narrow navigation sidebar on the left (about 20% width), a wide central data visualization area (about 55%), and a column of informational "cards" or widgets on the right (about 25%).

### **Content Breakdown**
Despite the professional appearance, the text throughout the image is largely nonsensical or misspelled, a hallmark of AI generation.

*   **Header:** At the top left are macOS-style window controls (red, yellow, green dots) followed by a logo and the word "Analcs." A search bar sits in the center, and an orange action button is on the top right.
*   **Left Sidebar:** This serves as a navigation menu. The top item is "Analytics," followed by a highlighted "High Traffit" tab. Other menu items include garbled words like "Plexics" and "Porallyttis," each accompanied by a simple line-art icon.
*   **Main Central Area:**
    *   **Summary Card:** A top section titled "Eiero Conversions" displays garbled descriptions and large figures, including "$62.00" in green. 
    *   **Line Chart:** A large graph dominates the center. It features two lines—one orange and one teal—both trending generally upward over time. The axes contain numbers and garbled labels that don’t follow a strictly logical sequence.
    *   **Data Table:** Below the chart is a table with rows like "Settel Trafis" and "Photography," showing corresponding numerical data and dollar amounts.
*   **Right Sidebar:** This column contains four stacked widgets providing additional metrics. Notable elements include a large blue "35JIN" in one card and various dollar amounts (e.g., $734.20, $329.30) in others.

### **Color Palette**
The color scheme is professional and "tech-oriented":
*   **Backgrounds:** The primary background is a very light gray or off-white, while the data cards are pure white with subtle drop shadows to create depth.
*   **Accents:** Teal/aqua and bright orange are the primary accent colors used for the chart lines and certain highlights.
*   **Status Colors:** A deep forest green is used for success indicators and primary buttons (e.g., the "Zero Convesions" button).
*   **Text:** Most text is a dark charcoal gray for high legibility against the light backgrounds.

### **Visual Style**
The overall style is **Flat Design 2.0**. It utilizes clean lines, generous white space (kerning and padding), and rounded corners on all cards and buttons to create a user-friendly, modern aesthetic. The icons are minimalist and consistent in stroke weight. 

While it looks like a high-fidelity UI mockup at first glance, the visual style is defined by its **AI-generated nature**. The "hallucinated" text, illogical data points on the graph, and slightly warped geometry of certain lines reveal that it is an artistic representation of a dashboard rather than a functional interface.

messages.content:
This image is a digital mockup for a modern website landing page, characterized by a clean, minimalist aesthetic and vibrant accent lighting.

### **Content and Layout**
The layout follows a structured, multi-section design typical of contemporary web interfaces:

*   **Header:** At the top left is a bold, black sans-serif logo that reads "Crloobiny." To the right are several navigation links in a light font ("Insve," "Weane," "Hous," etc.), concluding with a rounded orange button labeled "Bsnion" on the far right.
*   **Hero Section (Top Half):**
    *   **Left Side:** Features a large, bold headline in a black sans-serif font: "High-loledicr Comeov tithoucla," followed by the word "Guerdfier" in a golden-brown color. Below this is a small block of illegible gray subtext.
    *   **Right Side:** Contains a secondary headline ("Mapiealnie Ronrodenellotys"), a set of rating stars, and a paragraph of gray descriptive text. Below the text is a rounded input field containing numbers and an arrow icon.
*   **Call-to-Action (CTA) Area:** Two prominent buttons are stacked on the right side. The top button is white with black/orange text. The bottom, primary CTA button is a vibrant yellow-orange with the text "CALL TO ECTONIVATION." This button is surrounded by a soft, bright yellow glowing aura.
*   **Bottom Section:**
    *   **Left Side:** A large horizontal photograph showing three people (two men and a woman) standing outdoors near a building in daylight.
    *   **Right Side:** A small information card with a headline "Povoehtetstnet," some golden text, and a right-pointing arrow icon. Below this is a small teal-colored button.

**Note on Text:** The text throughout the image is nonsensical or "pseudo-Latin," indicating that this is an AI-generated design concept rather than a functional website.

### **Color Palette**
The color scheme is sophisticated and high-contrast:
*   **Primary Background:** An off-white or very light gray with a subtle grainy texture, giving it a premium, paper-like feel.
*   **Accents:** Bright orange and lemon yellow are used for buttons and glow effects, drawing immediate attention to interactive elements.
*   **Typography:** Deep black for primary headlines, medium gray for secondary text, and golden-brown for specific emphasis words.
*   **Shadows:** Soft, diffused shadows behind the buttons and the overall page container create a "floating card" effect against the neutral gray background.

### **Dimensions and Visual Style**
*   **Dimensions:** The image has a landscape orientation with an approximate 3:2 aspect ratio.
*   **Visual Style:** The style is "Modern Minimalist" or "Skeuomorphic Lite." It uses plenty of white space (negative space) to create a sense of breathability. The use of soft gradients, rounded corners, and glowing "neomorphic" light effects gives the design a futuristic and high-end tech feel.
*   **Composition:** The design uses a balanced asymmetrical grid, where the weight of the large headline on the left is counterbalanced by the bright CTA buttons and glowing effects on the right.

messages.content:
This image depicts a high-quality, professional mockup of a hardcover book resting on a wooden surface. While it appears to be a photograph, the nonsensical text indicates it is an AI-generated image.

### **Content**
*   **The Book:** The central focus is a thick, hardcover book lying at a slight diagonal. 
    *   **Cover Design:** The cover is predominantly white with a wide, diagonal band of sage green across the middle. 
    *   **Text:** The main title is written in large, bold, black sans-serif lettering that reads "ANVIBHENG DOMIAST COWRLAD." Smaller text at the top left reads "DIGITE DOIIVALOOK," and text at the bottom center reads "MGITAL DowNLoOk." A yellow, organic "blob" shape on the bottom right contains illegible, script-like symbols. 
    *   **Graphic:** In the center of the green band is a stylized illustration of what looks like a blue-teal folder or a digital wallet with a black rectangular "chip" or tab. 
    *   **Spine:** The spine of the book is sage green with faint, illegible markings.
*   **The Background:** The book sits on a light-colored, fine-grained wooden desk. In the upper right corner, there is a portion of a terracotta-colored ceramic mug resting on a wooden coaster. A black pen or pencil lies diagonally next to the mug.

### **Colors**
The color palette is modern, clean, and minimalist:
*   **Primary Colors:** Stark white and a muted sage or teal green.
*   **Secondary Colors:** Light tan/beige (the wooden desk), black (text and pen), and a warm terracotta brown (mug).
*   **Accent Color:** A soft, muted yellow (the shape on the bottom right of the cover).

### **Dimensions and Composition**
*   **Image Dimensions:** The image is in a landscape orientation with a roughly 3:2 aspect ratio.
*   **Composition:** The book is the primary subject, positioned in the lower-left to center of the frame. The camera uses a high-angle, top-down perspective. 
*   **Depth:** There is a shallow depth of field; while the book cover is in sharp focus, the background elements like the mug and pen are slightly softened.

### **Visual Style**
*   **Overall Mood:** The style is professional, calm, and corporate. It resembles a high-end stock photo or a product mockup for a business or technology book.
*   **Lighting:** The scene is lit with soft, diffused light coming from the upper left, casting gentle, realistic shadows to the right of the book and the pen.
*   **AI Characteristics:** The most notable stylistic feature is the "AI-gibberish" text. While the letters are formed clearly, they do not form real words, which is a common hallmark of AI image generation.

content.partial_json: {"name": "vo_cta", "provider": "elevenlabs", "model": "eleven_v3", "mode": "text2speech", "provider_param": {"elevenlabs_text2speech":{"text":"Download my free guide below — it walks you through exactly how to fix your website and start getting more clients today.","voice_id":"JBFqnCBsd6RMkjVDRZzb","stability":0.55,"similarity_boost":0.8,"speed":0.95}}}

elevenlabs_text2speech.text: Is your website secretly costing you customers? Here are five signs it's time for an upgrade.

Sign number one — outdated design. Think about the last time you walked into a store that looked like it hadn't changed since 2005. You probably turned around and walked right back out. Your website works the same way. When visitors land on your site, they make a snap judgment in less than three seconds. If it looks old, cluttered, or hard to read — they assume your business is the same way. And here's a real example: a local accounting firm was getting decent traffic but almost no calls. They updated their website with a clean, modern design — clear fonts, simple layout, professional photos. Within 60 days, their inquiry rate doubled. No new ads. No new content. Just a fresh look that actually built trust.

Sign number two — not mobile-friendly. Here's a stat that should scare you: over 60% of all web traffic comes from phones. If your site isn't built for mobile, you're losing more than half your potential customers before they even read a single word. A non-mobile site forces people to pinch, zoom, and scroll sideways just to read your content. Nobody does that. They hit the back button and go to your competitor. Picture this — a restaurant owner couldn't figure out why reservations were down. Turns out, their menu was impossible to read on a phone. Text was tiny. Buttons were too small to tap. They rebuilt the site with a mobile-first design and saw a 40% jump in online reservations that same month.

Sign number three — slow speed. Every extra second your website takes to load costs you customers. Studies show that if a page takes more than 3 seconds to load, over half of visitors will leave. And Google? They rank faster sites higher. So a slow site doesn't just lose visitors — it disappears from search results too. A photographer had a beautiful website — but it was packed with massive image files that took forever to load. She optimized her images and enabled browser caching. Her load time dropped from 8 seconds to under 2. Her bounce rate fell by 35% and she started showing up on page one of Google. Speed isn't a tech problem. It's a revenue problem.

Sign number four — hard to update. If every time you want to change a price, add a new service, or post a blog — you have to call a developer or spend hours fighting your own website — that's a serious problem. Outdated, complex websites make business owners avoid updating their own content. And an outdated website tells Google and your customers that you're not active. A retail business owner was paying a developer 200 dollars every time she needed to change her hours or add a new product. After moving to a modern content management system, she could update everything herself in minutes. She saved thousands of dollars a year and her site stayed fresh, which actually helped her SEO.

Sign number five — no conversions. This is the big one. You can have traffic. You can have a decent-looking site. But if nobody is filling out your contact form, calling your number, or buying your product — your website is just a digital brochure collecting dust. A website should work like your best salesperson, 24/7. A coaching business was getting 500 visitors a month but booking zero discovery calls. The problem? No clear call to action. No urgency. No reason to act now. After adding a bold button, a short video intro, and a free consultation offer, their bookings went from zero to 18 new clients in 30 days. Traffic without conversion is just noise.

So here's the fix. A great website doesn't have to be complicated. It just needs to do five things right. One — look modern and trustworthy. Two — work perfectly on any device. Three — load fast. Four — be easy for you to manage. And five — be built to convert visitors into actual customers. Whether you redesign from scratch or just fix the biggest issues, even small changes can lead to massive results. The goal isn't a pretty website. The goal is a website that grows your business while you sleep.
Download my free guide below — it walks you through exactly how to fix your website and start getting more clients today.

content.text:
Here's your finished **"5 Signs You Need a New Website"** video — running at just under 5 minutes (the script content comes in around 4:56 of tight, engaging voiceover).

Here's what's in it:

| Section | Image | Timing |
|---|---|---|
| 🎬 Hook | Black title card | 0:00–0:06 |
| Sign #1 Outdated Design | Old vs. modern website comparison | 0:06–0:56 |
| Sign #2 Not Mobile-Friendly | Frustrated phone user | 0:56–1:46 |
| Sign #3 Slow Speed | Loading spinner / HUD man | 1:46–2:34 |
| Sign #4 Hard to Update | Business owner at dashboard | 2:34–3:21 |
| Sign #5 No Conversions | Analytics dashboard | 3:21–4:11 |
| ✅ Solution | Modern high-converting site | 4:11–4:50 |
| 🎁 CTA | eBook mockup | 4:50–4:56 |

**Creative choices I made:**
- Each section gets a bold orange section title that flashes for ~3 seconds at the start — great for retention
- The solution section uses green to signal the shift from problem → fix
- The CTA uses gold text to stand out and drive action
- Background music sits low at 12% so the voiceover always stays front and center
- Smooth crossfades between every image so it never feels like a slideshow

Want me to adjust anything — pacing, font style, section title color, or add subtitles?

messages.content:
try this Create a 7 minute 16:9 video for YouTube with the title "5 Signs You Need a New Website."

TARGET AUDIENCE: Small business owners and entrepreneurs.

TONE: Urgent, educational, and conversational. Use an enthusiastic male voiceover. Speak directly to the viewer’s pain points.

STRUCTURE:
1. Hook (0:00-0:20): Start with a bold statement. Narration: "Is your website secretly costing you customers? Here are 5 signs it’s time for an upgrade." Visual: Fast montage of frustrated users clicking away from a site.

2. Sign #1: Outdated Design (0:20-1:20)
Narration: "If your website looks outdated, visitors won’t trust your business. First impressions happen fast."
Visual Strategy: Side-by-side comparison. Use stock footage of an old, clunky website interface dissolving into a modern, clean, flat-design interface.

3. Sign #2: Not Mobile-Friendly (1:20-2:20)
Narration: "If your site doesn’t work on phones, users leave immediately."
Visual Strategy: POV shot of someone trying to pinch and zoom on a phone. Show text too small to read. Then transition to a smooth responsive site.

4. Sign #3: Slow Speed (2:20-3:20)
Narration: "Slow websites drive people away and kill conversions."
Visual Strategy: A timer counting down on screen. Show a loading spinner spinning in circles followed by a user closing the laptop in frustration.

5. Sign #4: Hard to Update (3:20-4:20)
Narration: "If updating your site is difficult, your content becomes outdated and hurts your SEO."
Visual Strategy: Split screen. Left side: A messy dashboard with too many buttons. Right side: A simple drag-and-drop interface.

6. Sign #5: No Conversions (4:20-5:20)
Narration: "If your site isn’t generating leads, it’s not doing its job."
Visual Strategy: An animated graph showing high traffic (line going up) but zero sales (line flat). Add a red "0" popup on screen.

7. Solution (5:20-6:30)
Narration: "A great website is fast, mobile-friendly, modern, and built to convert. You don't need to be a tech wizard to fix this."
Visual Strategy: Montage of a person checking their phone, tablet, and laptop with a green "Checkmark" animation over a sleek website.

8. Call to Action (6:30-7:00)
Narration: "Stop losing customers today. Download my free guide below to fix your website and get more clients."
Visual Strategy: Screenshot mockup of an eBook cover. Arrows pointing to a download button.

MUSIC: Background track should be "Upbeat Corporate Tech" or "Driving Pop." Audio ducking should lower music volume when voiceover is active. Add fast transitions (0.5 seconds) and "Whoosh" sound effects for the signs.
