messages.content:
1	---
     2	name: generation-skill
     3	description: Execute video segment generation from a confirmed production plan. Handles model routing (Seedance / Kling / Grok — four-layer system), prompt writing, single/multi-sequence generation, and post-generation sanity checks. Produces video segments and hands off to assembly-skill for timeline assembly, audio production (VO/BGM), mixing, and cover. Use when production plan is confirmed and generation is needed. Not for creative direction (brainstorm-skill), production design (script-skill), subject assets (subject-asset-skill), or modifying existing videos (modification-skill).
     4	---
     5	
     6	# Usage
     7	
     8	**Input**: A locked production plan from script-skill (sequence breakdown, production model, strategy), the per-sequence reference map from subject-asset-skill, optionally a concept preview (with extracted key frames), supplementary reference assets, and optional reference image/video/audio from the user. **Note on images**: All images are plain references — no first-frame/end-frame distinction at the API level; describe first-frame intent in the prompt. **Audio strategy**: Audio co-generation is the #1 priority — SFX and on-screen dialogue are always co-generated in the video generation call, never post-produced. See **references/audio-strategy.md** and **references/assembly-rules.md** for assembly handoff blockers.
     9	
    10	**PREREQUISITE CHECK**: Before starting any generation, verify that the execution strategy is sufficiently specified, regardless of where it came from. At minimum, the following must be clear: hard user constraints, `shot_strategy`, `audio_strategy`, `consistency_strategy`, and `fallback_strategy`. If upstream skills did not provide them explicitly, create a lightweight internal plan before generation. Do not block solely because a specific skill or reference file was not read. Do block or pause if the execution strategy is materially under-specified. Generating sequences without a unified plan produces disjointed, inconsistent video.
    11	
    12	**STORYBOARD CONFIRMATION PROTOCOL** (applies when generation-skill operates directly from user brief — no locked plan from upstream skills):
    13	
    14	When the user provides a brief with reference images and format confirmation, and no upstream plan exists, follow this lightweight confirmation protocol before generating video:
    15	
    16	1. **Present the sequence plan as text** — include the Scene Reference → Sequence Mapping table (Rule 2) and a one-line description per sequence. Ask if the direction is OK. **Do not generate storyboard images by default** — text descriptions are sufficient for direction alignment. Only generate storyboard key frames if the user explicitly requests visual previews.
    17	
    18	2. **If storyboard images are generated**: Collect feedback. The user may confirm the direction, request scene changes, or flag character appearance issues. **Separate scene direction from character appearance**: if the user's feedback is solely about the character (not the scenes), fix the character reference image once and proceed to video generation — do not regenerate all storyboard frames.
    19	
    20	3. **Maximum 2 confirmation rounds**: After the user has confirmed the scene direction (even implicitly — e.g., feedback is only about character details, not scene content), proceed to video generation. Minor character adjustments (remove accessories, clean up details) should be resolved in a single character reference fix, not through additional storyboard rounds.
    21	
    22	4. **Anti-loop detection**: If 2+ consecutive user messages address only character appearance and not scene direction, the scene direction is implicitly confirmed. Consolidate all character feedback into one reference image fix, then immediately start video generation.
    23	
    24	**Output**: All generated video segments (with their sound-on/off status), plus the Voice Strategy Declaration and BGM Timing Mode Declaration as planning artifacts. These are handed off to **assembly-skill** for timeline assembly, audio production (TTS/BGM), mixing, cover bake-in, and final delivery. **Never expose internal production terms** (sequence numbers, generation modes, sound on/off, model names) to the user — use natural creative language.
    25	
    26	## Degraded Execution Declaration
    27	
    28	When using a non-ideal path due to model or tool limits, record it explicitly in working context before handoff.
    29	
    30	Examples:
    31	
    32	- `degraded_mode: segmented_continuation`
    33	- `degraded_mode: post_produced_voice`
    34	- `degraded_mode: weak_consistency_locking`
    35	
    36	A degraded path is allowed if it does not directly violate hard user constraints. The key requirement is to make the compromise visible so downstream assembly and QA can evaluate it correctly.
    37	
    38	**Constraints & Behavior**:
    39	
    40	1. **Model selection**: Execute using the model recommended by script-skill. The four-layer routing system, tool call mapping, and decision tree are defined in **references/video-models-scheduling.md** Section I — verify every `video_generate` call against that definitive reference before submission.
    41	   - **Pre-call model verification** (mandatory): Before every `video_generate` call, confirm `provider`, `model`, `mode`, and `provider_param` key all match the routing decision per the mapping table in **references/video-models-scheduling.md** Section I. Mismatching any field is a critical error.
    42	   - **Reference-discard check**: If user-uploaded reference materials exist and the call uses `mode: "text2video"`, STOP — `text2video` cannot accept `image_list`/`video_list`/`audio_list`. Re-route to Seedance or Kling with `reference2video`.
    43	   - **Image-list population check**: When user-uploaded images exist and the production model supports references, `image_list` MUST contain at least the user's primary reference image.
    44	   - Generation-skill only overrides the model on degradation (see Rule 12).
    45	2. **Build the Production Blueprint**: Break the brief into an executable **Sequence** list. When **Grok** is selected, the plan is a single sequence; write one flowing narrative prompt per Grok's style. Each sequence represents a **self-contained narrative paragraph containing multiple shots** (see **references/video-prompting.md**). Duration per sequence depends on the production model: **Seedance ≤15s**, **Kling ≤10s**, **Grok ≤10s**. **Single-call preference** (see **references/multi-sequence-strategy.md**): When total video duration fits within the model's single-call max, strongly prefer generating the entire video in one call — use prompt-internal timing cues (`[0–5s]`, `[5–10s]`, `[10–15s]`) to structure beats within a single prompt. This avoids unnecessary cross-segment consistency risk, redundant API calls, and post-assembly overhead. Split only when there is a clear production reason (independent scenes needing different references, isolated retry needs). For multi-sequence videos, leverage Seedance's 15s cap to reduce sequence count and cross-sequence consistency risk — let narrative rhythm determine natural break points within the model's limit. Plan all sequences upfront before generating any — map the full narrative arc first, then write individual prompts. End at a natural pause point so sequences can chain cleanly.
    46	   - **Shot duration heuristics** — when the sequence includes voiceover or on-screen text:
    47	     - **VO/text coverage**: Budget shot duration ≥ spoken/read duration + 0.5–1s slack (~3–4 Chinese chars/sec, ~2.5–3 English words/sec). When planning a sequence that hosts voiceover/narration, budget the sequence duration to be **at least** the estimated VO read time + 1s — never shorter. Example: a 20-character Chinese voiceover at 3–4 chars/sec needs 5–7s of speaking time; do not assign it to a 4s sequence. If the narrative structure constrains the sequence to a shorter duration, shorten the VO text to fit the available time.
    48	     - **Breathing room**: ~0.5–1s between narrated shots, ~1.5–2s visual-only between narrated sequences.
    49	     - **Shots without narration** follow emotional weight rather than text timing — see `../script-skill/references/editing-rhythm-design.md` Section 4.
    50	     - Sub-second precision is below model resolution — use timing cues as pacing guidance.
    51	   - **Visual Intent Pass** (multi-sequence only): Before writing any individual prompt, establish a unified visual language using the **Visual Style Convention** (4 constants: rendering style, color palette, lighting mood, texture quality) from the script-skill handoff. These constants become a **style preamble** prepended to every segment's prompt — this prevents each segment from independently interpreting the visual style. Verify adjacent segments for unintentional style drift before writing prompts.
    52	   - **Scene Reference Image Coverage** (mandatory when user provides multiple distinct scene/environment reference images):
    53	
    54	     | Constant | Example | What it controls |
    55	     |----------|---------|-----------------|
    56	     | **Rendering style** | photorealistic / cinematic / painterly / illustration / motion graphics | The fundamental look — mixing styles across segments (e.g., nature macro → CGI → studio) is the #1 cause of visual disconnection |
    57	     | **Color palette** | warm golden tones / cool desaturated / neutral with jade accents | Dominant color character — shifts of >2 stops between adjacent segments look like different videos |
    58	     | **Lighting mood** | soft natural / dramatic rim light / studio even / golden hour | Lighting consistency is as important as color — a studio-lit segment adjacent to a natural-light segment breaks immersion |
    59	     | **Texture quality** | organic film grain / clean digital / dreamy soft focus | Surface treatment — gritty next to polished reads as a style error, not variety |
    60	
    61	     These 4 constants become a **style preamble** that is prepended to every segment's prompt. Example: "Cinematic photorealistic look, warm golden palette with jade accents, soft diffused natural lighting, organic film-like texture." This preamble is not optional — it is the mechanism that prevents each segment from independently interpreting the visual style.
    62	
    63	     **Step 2 — Visual Intent Table** — one row per sequence:
    64	
    65	     | Seq | Color Temperature | Shot Anchor | Human Relationship | Transition In | Narrative Justification |
    66	     |-----|-------------------|-------------|--------------------|---------------|-------------------------|
    67	     | ... | ...               | ...         | ...                | ...           | ...                     |
    68	
    69	     Rules for filling the table:
    70	     - Every cell must contain an explicit absolute description (e.g., "cool overcast grey", "wide → mid-shot", "two workers in conversation"). No cell may be left blank or marked "same as above."
    71	     - Adjacent rows may share the same value when the narrative calls for continuity — but the justification cell must explain why (e.g., "maintains emotional momentum from seq2"). Similarity by default, not by intent, is what this step prevents.
    72	     - Transitions: carry meaning beyond cut/no-cut — note the reason (e.g., "dissolve — 20-year time jump", "cut — same scene, momentum continuity").
    73	     - **Color Temperature** values must be consistent with the Style Convention's color palette. If a segment needs a different temperature (e.g., flashback in cool blue), the Narrative Justification must explain the shift, and the transition must signal it.
    74	     - This table is produced once before any generation call. Because every row is self-contained with absolute values, it works identically for both serial and parallel generation.
    75	
    76	     **Step 3 — Style Drift Check** (validate before writing prompts): Scan the completed table row by row. For each adjacent pair of segments, verify:
    77	     - Would the two segments look like they belong in the same video? If segment A describes "macro nature photography in soft morning light" and segment B describes "CGI particle convergence in dark studio lighting," they will look like clips from two different projects.
    78	     - If a style shift is intentional (e.g., reality → fantasy transition), the Transition In cell must contain a transition type that signals the shift (dissolve, whiteout, motivated cut) and the Narrative Justification must explain why.
    79	     - If a style shift is unintentional — the segments just ended up with different visual languages because they describe different subjects — **unify them** by rewriting both to share the Style Convention's constants. Different subjects can coexist within one visual language; different visual languages cannot.
    80	
    81	     Prompts are written by translating each row's design decisions into language — not by re-inventing the visual design during prompt writing. **Every prompt starts with the Style Convention preamble**, followed by the segment-specific direction from the table row.
    82	   - **Scene Reference Image Coverage** (mandatory when user provides multiple distinct scene/environment reference images):
    83	     Count the number of distinct scene/environment reference images the user provided (different locations, backgrounds, or settings — not multiple angles of the same scene). The Production Blueprint must **use every user-provided scene reference image** — no image may be silently dropped.
    84	     - **Mapping is flexible**: N scene reference images do NOT require N sequences. A single ≤10s sequence can contain multiple scene transitions — pass multiple scene reference images into one sequence's `image_list` and describe the scene cuts in the prompt. Kling handles intelligent scene-splitting from text. Example: 3 scene references in a 10s sequence → prompt describes a 3-location montage, image_list contains all 3 scene images plus the character reference.
    85	     - **Sequence count is driven by duration and narrative, not reference count**: Plan sequences based on `target_duration / max_sequence_duration` and narrative arc, then distribute scene references across sequences. A 15s video with 4 scene references could be 2 sequences × ~8s (each covering 2 scenes) or 4 sequences × ~4s — the right split depends on pacing and transition design.
    86	     - **Explicit mapping table** (include in plan output to user):
    87	       | Seq | Scene References Used | Planned Duration |
    88	       |-----|----------------------|-----------------|
    89	       | 1   | Image 1 (street), Image 2 (station) | 8s |
    90	       | 2   | Image 3 (bridge), Image 4 (temple) | 8s |
    91	     - If any user-provided scene reference image is not mapped to a sequence, explain why in the plan.
    92	     - This rule supplements, not replaces, the Visual Intent Table — the mapping table is a planning tool; the Visual Intent Table governs visual consistency.
    93	   - **Speed-change segments**: When the creative brief calls for slow-motion or fast-motion within a segment, generate source material at the correct pre-speed duration: source duration = output duration × playback speed. Example: 5s of 0.5x slow-motion needs only 2.5s of source → generate with duration=3 (round up), then apply `speed=0.5` in edit spec using the full source (`source.in_ms=0`, omit `source.out_ms` or set it to the full source duration). Do not generate full-length source and then trim `source.out_ms` to fit — truncation discards the tail of the action, often the most important moment.
    94	   - **Voice Strategy Declaration** (mandatory before generation — any video with spoken audio): Before writing any generation prompt, classify every spoken audio element in the creative brief and assign it to exactly one source. This declaration is binding — once a line is assigned to a source, it cannot also be produced by the other source.
    95	    - **Step 1 — Classify each spoken element** into one of: **on-screen dialogue**, **off-screen narration**, **inner monologue**, or **SFX/atmosphere**. Read the brief through the same three lenses used upstream: visible presenter speech, voiceover, and mixed. If the speaker boundary is still unclear, pause and ask one focused question before any generation call.
    96	     - **Step 2 — Determine the voice routing** based on on-screen visibility and whether voice identity must be preserved across languages:
    97	       - **On-screen dialogue / monologue** (character visibly speaks — any visual style including 2D animation, illustration, motion graphics, realistic): **Co-generate path.** Include the dialogue/monologue in the sequence prompt (`sound: on`). Co-generation produces lip-synced, emotionally matched voice that is synchronized with the character's visible mouth movements — this cannot be replicated by post-assembly TTS regardless of visual style. Modern video models handle audio-visual co-generation well across all visual styles.
    98	      - **On-screen presenter with supplied speech audio or voice reference**: Start with audio-conditioned generation rather than silent footage plus post voice overlay. Seedance-style generation often fits best. Pass the speech clip via `audio_list` with the presenter references. For multi-sequence videos, split speech by sequence first (≤15s per chunk) and feed each sequence only its matching chunk.
    99	       - **Off-screen narration / voiceover** (no on-screen speaker): **TTS path.** `sound: on` for SFX only in the prompt (no speech). Narration via `audio_produce` in post-assembly. This gives full voice control (consistent voice ID, pacing, tone) for narration that spans multiple sequences.
   100	       - **Mixed: same video has both on-screen dialogue AND off-screen narration**: **Split path.** On-screen dialogue lines: co-generate in prompts. Off-screen narration: TTS. The voice difference between co-generated dialogue and TTS narration is acceptable — they serve different narrative roles (character voice vs. narrator voice).
   101	       - **Inner monologue with no visible lip movement** (character thinking, no mouth movement): **TTS path.** Treat as off-screen narration since there is no visual speaking action to synchronize with.
   102	       - **Cross-language dubbing with voice preservation** (user wants the target-language speech to keep the same speaker / same character timbre as the source audio): **Reference-voice dubbing path.** Do **not** route this as ordinary generic TTS. First extract or isolate the source speech and treat it as a **voice identity reference anchor**. Then, if tooling supports it, generate the target-language speech with voice-clone / reference-conditioned TTS. If tooling does not support true voice preservation, explicitly tell the user before final delivery — never silently downgrade this request to arbitrary target-language TTS.
   103	     - **Step 3 — Record the declaration** (internal, not shown to user): For each sequence, note which spoken lines go into the prompt (co-generate), which are produced via `audio_produce` (TTS), and which are absent (SFX-only sequences). This declaration feeds directly into prompt writing (Rule 7) and the Assembly Handoff (Rule 10).
   104	     - **Anti-Doubling Hard Stop**: A spoken line MUST have exactly one audio source — enforced at **planning time**, not just assembly time. Before triggering any generation or TTS call, verify that each spoken line is assigned to exactly one source in the Step 3 declaration. If the same line appears in both a generation prompt (co-gen) and an `audio_produce` call (TTS), this is a planning error — cancel one path before any call is made. At assembly time: if both co-generated voice (embedded in video) and a TTS asset exist for the same spoken content, assembly is **blocked**. Remove one source before proceeding. See **references/assembly-rules.md** Pre-Assembly Checklist item 6.
   105	     - **Dubbing downgrade hard stop**: Requests for “same voice”, “same speaker”, “same character voice”, “keep the original timbre”, or “translate this speech into another language but keep the same voice” are **not equivalent** to ordinary narration requests. If the pipeline cannot perform reference-voice dubbing, stop and surface the limitation to the user instead of silently shipping a generic TTS voice.
   106	   - **BGM Timing Mode Declaration** (mandatory for any video with BGM — decide before generation begins): Determine the temporal relationship between music and visuals. This decision affects generation order.
   107	     - **Step 1 — Assess the creative brief's audio-visual relationship**:
   108	       - Does the brief describe specific musical moments tied to visual events (beat drops on scene changes, rhythmic cuts, music swells at emotional peaks)? → **video-first**: generate video segments first, analyze their actual rhythm, then compose BGM to match.
   109	       - Does the user provide an audio track, or does the brief design the narrative around a musical arc (music video, dance choreography, audio-driven montage)? → **music-first**: generate or analyze the audio first, extract beat structure, then generate video segments with timing informed by the audio. **Seedance `audio_list` implementation** (mandatory when Seedance is the production model): pass the user's audio file via the `audio_list` parameter on the `video_generate` call (use the audio asset path returned by upstream tools) — Seedance generates video whose motion and rhythm are reactive to the input audio. This is the core mechanism of music-first mode. Do NOT generate video without `audio_list` and then mix the audio in via `execute_edit_video` — that produces unsynchronized results. When the user asks to use the feeling/rhythm of an uploaded music track or provides BGM as creative input, this path is triggered.
   110	       - Is the BGM purely ambient atmosphere with no expected sync to visual beats (background texture, mood layer, simple product showcase)? → **independent**: generate BGM and video in parallel for speed.
   111	     - **Step 2 — Record the mode** (internal): Note the chosen mode and reasoning. This feeds directly into execution order (Rule 5 for multi-sequence generation) and the Assembly Handoff (Rule 10).
   112	     - **Default**: For multi-sequence narrative videos (>10s), default to **video-first** unless the brief explicitly indicates music-led pacing. For single-segment videos (≤10s), default to **independent**. **Exception**: When the user provides an audio track (uploaded BGM/music), default to **music-first** regardless of duration or sequence count.
   113	     - See **references/assembly-rules.md** "BGM Generation Timing Strategy" for the full framework.
   114	3. **Reference Mapping per Sequence** (mandatory before generation begins): Using the Subject Element Registry from subject-asset-skill, build a **per-sequence reference plan** — which assets go into each sequence's image list, and what role each plays.
   115	   - For each sequence, identify which subject elements from the registry appear in that sequence's narrative.
   116	   - Assign concrete assets: user uploads, concept preview key frames, supplementary generated images. Each asset gets a positional index (`image 1`, `image 2`, ...) that will be referenced in the prompt.
   117	   - **Every image in the image list must be explicitly referenced in the prompt** — declare its role (product_ref, character_ref, style_ref, etc.) and what the model should extract from it. No orphaned references.
   118	   - **Gap filling**: If the registry shows an element as "uncovered" or "text description," write a detailed textual description for it in the prompt. Use character budget on uncovered elements; save budget on covered elements by pointing to reference images instead of re-describing them.
   119	   - **When image slots are limited** (see tool schema for exact capacity per scenario), prioritize: product_ref > character_ref > style_ref > environment_ref > packaging_ref. If a sequence needs more references than the limit allows, combine roles (one image can anchor both product and style) or compress lower-priority elements to text descriptions.
   120	   - **User upload consistency anchor — never text2image what the user already provided**: When the user has uploaded reference images (character, scene, style, product), these images are the **single source of visual truth**. All derived assets must trace back to user uploads to maintain consistency:
   121	     - **Permitted**: Directly use user uploads in `image_list` — highest consistency.
   122	     - **Permitted**: Use `image2image` with a user upload as the source image (e.g., to place the character into a scene, to remove accessories, to adjust framing) — maintains consistency because the source image anchors the visual identity.
   123	     - **Prohibited**: Use `text2image` to regenerate content that a user upload already covers — text descriptions cannot capture the full visual identity of the user's asset, and the model will produce a different interpretation. This applies to both character references and scene references. Even if the prompt describes the user's image in great detail, the text2image output will diverge from the original.
   124	    - **When a referenced asset becomes unresolved**: Refresh the asset metadata/reference via `get_file_info`; do not regenerate the asset via `text2image`. Reference-resolution failures are transient infrastructure issues, not a reason to create a new (inconsistent) version of an existing asset.
   125	  - **Refresh file info before generation calls**: Before issuing any batch of `video_generate` or `image_generate` calls that reference uploaded assets, call `get_file_info` for all referenced assets so the tool sees fresh `file` inputs. Do not rely on stale cached file info — unresolved file paths/inputs can cause avoidable "file not found" failures and unnecessary regeneration.
   126	4. **Single-Sequence (≤10s Kling/Grok, ≤15s Seedance)**: Use **create video** with reference images and **audio co-generation enabled** (`sound: "on"` for Seedance, `sound: on` for Kling) to produce video + synchronized audio together — **dialogue, monologue, and SFX/environment/atmosphere** are co-generated. **Music and voiceover/narration may also be co-generated only when the final user-facing output is this one single segment** and there is no expected later assembly, extension, or revision chain. If the segment may later be reused, extended, or assembled into a longer cut, keep music and voiceover in post. **Never split a single-sequence video into multiple calls**. Seedance supports `sound: "on"` even when `image_list`, `video_list`, or `audio_list` are provided — unlike Kling which forces silent output with reference video.
   127	  - **User-provided audio check** (mandatory for single-sequence Seedance): If the user uploaded audio (BGM, music, sound reference, speech reference, or a pre-generated spoken clip) and the production model is Seedance, include the audio file in `audio_list` when that audio is meant to drive the final visible performance. Seedance generates video with motion and rhythm reactive to the input audio — this produces superior audio-visual sync compared to mixing the audio in afterward via `execute_edit_video`. When `audio_list` is provided for music-first mode, the prompt should NOT include `"no background music"` — the audio_list IS the music source, and the model should generate visuals synchronized to it.
   128	   - **Key-frame control is optional, not default**: For a single-sequence reference-based video, do **not** automatically generate supplementary key frames. First attempt direct production using the user uploads as `character_ref` / `product_ref` / `scene_ref` and describe the state change in the video prompt.
   129	   - **When a control key frame is justified**: Generate **one** supplementary key frame only when it materially reduces drift risk in a critical beat — e.g. a complex final worn state, assembled product payoff, transformation target state, or another high-stakes end frame whose visual relationship is not directly covered by the user uploads.
   130	   - **Single-sequence key-frame cap**: Default cap is **0–1** supplementary key frames for a single-sequence video. Go beyond one only when multiple distinct high-risk visual states genuinely need independent control.
   131	   - **Reference-led hard rule for supplementary key frames**: If the key frame concerns a subject/product already present in user uploads, the key frame must be derived from those uploads via direct reference inputs (`image2image` / reference-led image generation). **Never** generate that key frame via pure `text2image`.
   132	5. **Multi-Sequence (>15s Seedance / >10s Kling)**: See **references/multi-sequence-strategy.md** for the full parallel/serial decision framework. **Parallel is the default** for speed. Serial is only for continuous unbroken action or same-scene frame-level continuity. Audio is **not** a factor — Seedance supports `sound: "on"` in serial continuation.
   133	   - **User intent overrides**: Explicit requests for fast cuts / montage / multi-angle → parallel regardless of action type.
   134	   - **Parallel**: All sequences use create video with images only, `sound: "on"`. Sequences generated concurrently with shared reference images.
   135	  - **Speech-audio-driven presenter exception**: When a visible presenter follows supplied/generated speech audio, Seedance is often the strongest starting point. Cut the speech into per-sequence chunks before generation and attach the matching chunk to each sequence's `audio_list`; reusing one full speech track usually weakens alignment.
   136	   - **Serial continuation**: Seedance: pass previous sequence as `video_list`, `sound: "on"`, ≤15s. Kling: reference video forces silent — prefer converting to parallel with extracted key frames to preserve audio. Use relative camera motion descriptions across segments.
   137	   - **Tail-frame extraction**: Extract a **static image** (PNG/JPEG) from the sharpest frame in the final ~0.5s — not a video clip. Never pass MP4 into `image_list`. Never AI-regenerate the extracted frame.
   138	   - **Parallel prerequisite — reference completeness check**: Before parallel generation, verify every cross-sequence subject element has a concrete visual reference. `style_ref` only counts for look — it does NOT count as character/environment identity coverage. If any element lacks a reference → generate it first.
   139	   - **Single-segment fallback**: If one segment fails, fall back only that segment per the degradation chain. Do not switch all remaining segments.
   140	   - **EXECUTION MANDATE**: When parallel mode is selected, **issue all generation calls simultaneously in a single tool-call batch**. Never serialize independent calls. This applies to concept previews and image generation batches too.
   141	   - **Audio handoff**: After all segments are generated, hand off to assembly-skill. SFX and on-screen dialogue are **always co-generated** — never post-produced. Do not re-generate co-generated audio; do not co-generate lines assigned to TTS.
   142	6. **Reference Anchor Input** (when using Seedance or Kling with reference images): If a concept preview exists, **extract a representative key frame as a static image** and treat it as the **`style_ref` for every production sequence** — this is the visual style anchor that establishes color grading, lighting tone, and overall aesthetic. Pass it alongside the `product_ref` / `character_ref` images in every sequence's image_list. **The product reference image alone constrains content (what appears), not style (how it looks) — without the preview frame as style_ref, each sequence will independently interpret the visual style and produce an incoherent look across the video.** Extract the frame at the midpoint of the preview clip (where the visual tone is most established), not from the first or last frame (which may be transitional). **This frame must appear in every sequence's image_list, not just the first.** **Critical**: the image list only accepts image files (JPEG, PNG, WebP) — **never pass a video asset into the image list**; this will cause "Image format is invalid" errors. When only a video asset is available for reference, either: (a) use it as the **reference video** input (feature mode, ≤10s), or (b) generate a static key frame image first, then pass the image. Do not pass a Grok-generated video directly as reference to Seedance or Kling — use extracted key frames instead. When references cannot cover all elements, generate supplementary reference images **before** starting generation — strong reference coverage is essential since parallel is the default mode. When using **Grok** for production (pure T2V, no assets), this step does not apply.
   143	   - **Do not over-credit the style anchor**: A preview key frame or generated style frame stabilizes color, lighting, and mood. It does **not** reliably stabilize who the character is, what a recurring room specifically looks like, or how two people relate in a story beat. Treat `style_ref` as necessary but insufficient for story-driven multi-sequence generation.
   144	   - **Single-still bootstrap prohibition**: Do not replace missing `character_ref` / `environment_ref` coverage by generating one generic style still and feeding it to every sequence. That pattern produces globally coherent color but locally generic scenes, drifting faces, and weak payoff shots.
   145	   - **Completed-look key frame interpretation**: A generated or extracted "completed look" key frame is a **control anchor for a specific target state**, not a replacement for the original `character_ref` or `product_ref`. Keep the user uploads in the image list and assign distinct roles in the prompt.
   146	   - **No silent reference break after `get_file_info`**: If `get_file_info` was called in preparation for a supplementary `image_generate`, the next `image_generate` call must actually include the referenced images as tool inputs. Do not refresh file info and then fall back to prompt-only `text2image` for the same covered subject.
   147	7. **Prompt Writing**: Follow **references/video-prompting.md** for the full prompt spec (layer priority, syntax per model, character limits, reference-first economy). Key execution rules unique to generation:
   148	   - **Material reference syntax**: Seedance: positional (`"image 1"`, `"video 2"`). Kling: `<filename>`. Grok: `@filename`. Every image in the list must be referenced in the prompt with its role declared.
   149	   - **SFX mandatory check**: Every `sound: on` prompt must contain at least one concrete SFX description tied to a visual action. Generic atmosphere words do not count.
   150	   - **No co-generated BGM**: Every `sound: on` prompt in multi-sequence production must include `"no background music, no musical score, no musical instruments — sound effects and ambient audio only"`. **Exception**: when `audio_list` is provided (music-first mode), do NOT include this exclusion.
   151	   - **SFX coherence** (multi-sequence): Define a shared ambient foundation across all segments. Plan SFX as a designed arc, not random per-segment choices.
   152	   - **Character consistency**: Pass the same reference images to every sequence featuring the character. Describe only motion/expression/pose in prompt — not static visual attributes visible in the reference.
   153	   - **Brand logo avoidance**: Include `"no visible brand logos, no trademark symbols, plain unbranded clothing"` when generating characters with clothing/accessories.
   154	   - **Product scale anchoring**: Every prompt with product-and-person interaction must contain a realistic scale statement.
   155	   - **On-screen text / packaging**: Follow `references/packaging-keyframe-guide.md`. Never ask the video model to typeset readable text from prompt — exact in-scene text belongs in a key frame reference image. Simple post-production text (subtitles, short titles, CTA, low-density callouts) is handled later by assembly via `kind=subtitle` or safe `kind=text`; dense data/price/contact overlays remain unsupported unless a concrete edit path is confirmed.
   156	8. **Per-Sequence Duration Enforcement**: When the user provides a shot-by-shot script with explicit per-segment timing (e.g., "[0-2s]", "[2-4s]"), each sequence's generation duration must match the scripted segment duration — **not** the model's default or maximum. After each sequence is generated, verify its actual duration via `ffprobe` or `get_file_info`. If a sequence exceeds the scripted duration, trim it in the edit spec with `source.in_ms` / `source.out_ms` to match the user's timing before assembly. Do not silently deliver a video longer than the agreed total — the sum of all trimmed sequences must match the user-confirmed duration (±1s tolerance).
   157	9. **Post-Generation Sanity Check** (multi-sequence, same character): After all sequences are generated and before assembly, do a **lightweight correctness check** — not a subjective quality review. Use `analyze_file_content` on one representative frame per sequence (mid-point) and verify only binary pass/fail criteria:
   158	   - **Character present**: Does the character actually appear in the sequence? (Catches cases where the model ignored the reference entirely.)
   159	   - **Gross deformation**: Is the character's body structurally intact? (Catches severe artifacts like missing limbs, extra heads, melted faces — not subtle style drift.)
   160	   - If either check fails on a sequence, re-generate that sequence once with the same reference images. Do not iterate beyond one retry — if the re-generation also fails the check, proceed with the best result and let the user decide.
   161	   - Do **not** check for subjective quality (expression accuracy, motion smoothness, aesthetic preference) — that is the user's judgment, not a gate. The goal is to catch obvious errors without adding meaningful latency to the pipeline.
   162	10. **Assembly Handoff** (after all video segments are generated and sanity-checked): Hand off to **assembly-skill** with:
   163	  - All generated video segments (`file` must use the actual asset path returned by upstream tools; include each segment's actual duration and sound-on/off status)
   164	   - Voice Strategy Declaration (which spoken lines are co-generated, which need TTS production)
   165	   - BGM Timing Mode Declaration (video-first / music-first / independent)
   166	   - Creative script audio direction (VO copy, BGM descriptions, SFX notes)
   167	   - Transition recommendations from the production plan
   168	   - Cover source candidates (concept preview key frames, style_ref images)
   169	   
   170	   Assembly-skill owns: TTS/VO production, BGM production, timeline assembly with transitions, audio mixing and volume management, duration enforcement, cover bake-in, and non-generative edits. See assembly-skill for all edit spec, mixing, and post-production rules.
   171	   
   172	   **Explicit voiceover copy is binding**: When the user or creative brief provides actual voiceover/narration lines, those are required deliverables that assembly-skill must produce. Do not let them be silently dropped during handoff.
   173	   
   174	   **No silent downgrade**: If voice or music generation is unavailable or fails, surface the limitation to the user rather than silently delivering an incomplete video.
   175	11. **Scenario Routing**: Route each generation call based on the production model and input. See **references/video-models-scheduling.md** Section II for model-specific scenarios and capacity limits.
   176	   - **Handoff first**: Start from the upstream production-model choice and only change course when there is a clear degradation reason from Rule 12.
   177	   - **Closeup guidance**: A closeup, hand detail, face detail, product beauty shot, or general precision request does not automatically call for Kling.
   178	   - **Preview continuity**: If the approved preview worked on Seedance and the same stylized identity and reference set remain in use, Seedance is often still the best production route unless a concrete issue appears.
   179	   - **Creating new footage** → create video. Seedance: `sound: "on"` regardless of reference type. Kling: sound on without reference video; always silent with reference video.
   180	   - **Continuing from a previous sequence** → Seedance: pass as `video_list` + `sound: "on"` (audio preserved). Kling: pass as reference video (always silent — prefer converting to parallel with ref images if audio must be preserved).
   181	   - **Visual-only fixes** (restyle, re-skin) → edit video (Kling only) — preserves structure and timing, sound off (can preserve original audio).
   182	   - **Reference video provided by user** → Seedance: use as `video_list` with `sound: "on"`. Kling: use as reference video, accept silent output.
   183	12. **Fallback & Degradation**: When the selected production model fails, follow the degradation chain in **references/video-models-scheduling.md** Section VII. Key principles:
   184	   - **Audio co-generation must be preserved** — never degrade to post-production SFX or dialogue. If audio co-gen fails: retry with reference images only (extract key frames from video), then change strategy and inform user.
   185	   - **Document the reason before switching models** — if execution moves from Seedance to Kling, note the concrete trigger in the working state before the next `video_generate` call. Reasons like `closeup`, `real face`, `detail`, or `better quality` are usually too vague on their own; valid triggers include copyrighted-IP rejection, readable text/UI requirements, micro-detail accuracy, or a documented Seedance generation failure.
   186	   - **Reference preservation is a hard rule** — when converting from reference video to reference images during degradation, always extract key frames from the video or use user originals. Never AI-regenerate. Never lose visual consistency.
   187	   - **Sequence splitting on Kling degradation** — when a Seedance sequence (≤15s) must degrade to Kling (max 10s), split into 2+ Kling-compatible sequences, prefer parallel for audio preservation.
   188	   - **Inform user** on significant mid-production changes — briefly, in natural language, not technical terms.
   189	13. **Image Prompt Preflight** (before every `image_generate` call):
   190	   - **Technical parameters never belong in prompt text**: Do **not** include aspect ratios (`9:16`, `16:9`), pixel sizes (`1920x1080`), quality labels (`4K`, `HD`), hex/RGB color codes, internal IDs, case numbers, or other metadata in the prompt. These tokens are frequently rendered into the image as visible text.
   191	   - **Provider-specific aspect handling is mandatory**: For Seedream, pass only mapped pixel sizes (`"1920x1080"`, `"1080x1920"`, `"1024x1024"`) via `size`; never pass ratio strings. For Gemini, pass ratio strings via `aspect_ratio`; never pass pixel dimensions there. Follow `references/image-generation-guide.md` exactly.
   192	   - **Natural-language color descriptions only**: Replace `#000840` / `rgb(...)` style values with visual wording like `deep navy blue`, `warm amber`, or `soft jade green`.
   193	   - **Final prompt sanity test**: Read the prompt once as if every token might be printed on the final image. If any remaining token would be embarrassing or obviously non-visual when rendered literally, rewrite before calling the tool.
   194	   - **Brand/IP name prohibition in text2image**: Never include trademarked character or brand names (e.g., Labubu, Pop Mart, Hello Kitty, Disney, Sanrio, Pokémon) in `text2image` prompts — these consistently trigger content safety false positives across providers (observed: >60% failure rate with Seedream). Replace with purely visual descriptions. Note: when the user has uploaded the character image, `text2image` should not be used at all per Rule 3 — use `image2image` with the user's upload as the source, or pass the upload directly.
   195	
   196	14. **Asset Organization** (before generation):
   197	   - When assets exceed the tool's capacity limits per scenario, classify material vs. reference, budget material first, compress references to minimum effective dose.
   198	   - **Seedance limits**: ≤9 images in `image_list`, ≤3 videos in `video_list` (total ≤15s), ≤3 audios in `audio_list` (total ≤15s, requires at least 1 image or video). Seedance also supports audio references — when the user provides BGM or audio, pass as `audio_list` for audio-visual sync.
   199	   - **Kling limits**: ≤7 images (no ref video) or ≤4 images (with ref video). Only 1 reference video per call, **must be ≤10s**. Over-length videos must be trimmed.
   200	   - When the user provides multiple videos, select the most relevant one; extract key frames from others to use as reference images.

messages.name: read_file

messages.content:
1	---
     2	name: script-skill
     3	description: "Sequence-level production design — break a creative script into executable shot sequences with complete visual, audio, editing, and effects design. Handles shot composition, camera language, SFX/dialogue/VO/music planning, visual packaging, production model routing (four-layer system), and production strategy. Use when a creative direction is locked and needs to be translated into a production-ready shot list. Not for initial creative exploration (brainstorm-skill), subject asset organization (subject-asset-skill), or video generation execution (generation-skill)."
     4	---
     5	
     6	# Usage
     7	
     8	**Input**: A creative script from brainstorm-skill — narrative arc, key beats, emotional shape, audio direction, visual style notes, plus user-uploaded reference assets and format preferences.
     9	
    10	**Output**: A production-ready **sequence breakdown** with per-shot design covering visuals, audio, editing rhythm, packaging, asset mapping, and model routing — carried forward as internal context for subject-asset-skill and generation-skill. This is the definitive execution plan: generation-skill follows it, not re-invents it.
    11	
    12	## Required Planning Output
    13	
    14	Before downstream production begins, produce a lightweight structured plan containing:
    15	
    16	- `constraint_summary`
    17	- `shot_strategy`
    18	- `audio_strategy`
    19	- `consistency_strategy`
    20	- `fallback_strategy`
    21	
    22	Definitions:
    23	
    24	- `constraint_summary`: separate **hard constraints**, **soft preferences**, and **negotiable details**.
    25	- `shot_strategy`: define how the final viewing experience is organized visually, including whether the piece is executed as a single generation, segmented continuation, or another valid production path.
    26	- `audio_strategy`: define how dialogue, narration, ambience, and music are handled, including whether audio is co-generated, post-produced, or mixed.
    27	- `consistency_strategy`: define how identity, style, lighting, color, costume/material state, and cross-sequence continuity are maintained.
    28	- `fallback_strategy`: define what degraded path is acceptable if model or tool limits prevent the ideal execution path.
    29	
    30	If target duration exceeds the single-generation limit of the selected model, segmented generation is allowed. In that case, explicitly define how continuity will be preserved across segments rather than assuming downstream skills will infer it.
    31	
    32	**Skip condition**: When the user provides a complete shot-by-shot brief with timing, reference mapping, and audio direction already specified, route to generation-skill directly.
    33	
    34	**CRITICAL — This skill is not optional**: When brainstorm-skill confirms a direction (from concept previews or text summary), **this skill must be completed before any generation begins**. Do not hand off to generation-skill with only the concept preview and the user's choice — generation-skill requires the full production plan. Skipping this step causes generation-skill to improvise the narrative structure, miss reference gaps, and produce inconsistent multi-segment videos.
    35	
    36	---
    37	
    38	# Core Workflow
    39	
    40	## Step 1 — Production Model Routing (four-layer system)
    41	
    42	Determine the production model based on user assets and creative requirements. This decision constrains all downstream planning (sequence duration, audio co-generation, prompt limits). The routing must land on a concrete production choice.
    43	
    44	### Routing Decision Tree
    45	
    46	Analyze user assets via multimodal analysis for copyrighted IP and reference precision needs:
    47	
    48	| Layer | Model | Provider/Param | When |
    49	|-------|-------|----------------|------|
    50	| **L1** | Grok | `fal` / `fal_grok_text2video` | Non-final pure T2V without precision needs, NSFW, or speed-priority. **Never valid when user-uploaded reference materials exist.** |
    51	| **L2** | Seedance Fast | `seedance` / `seedance_reference2video` | Quality previews, default pure T2V |
    52	| **L3** | Seedance | `seedance` / `seedance_reference2video` | **Production default** — text-driven, multimodal mixed, long narrative |
    53	| **L4** | Kling | `kling` / `kling_reference2video` | Copyrighted IP, readable in-frame text/UI, or selective micro-detail |
    54	
    55	### Routing Rules
    56	
    57	- **Default is Seedance** for nearly all reference-based and text-only cases (including real human face photos via subject reference).
    58	- **Grok exclusion**: Grok only supports `text2video` with no `image_list`/`video_list`/`audio_list`. Selecting Grok discards all user references. Only available for pure-text input.
    59	- **Face handling**: Seedance accepts faces (real human photos, illustration, anime, cartoon, AI-generated stylized characters) directly via subject reference. Pass the user's image into `image_list` unchanged. Do not pre-process faces into line art, do not mask or remove the face area, and do not auto-route to Kling on the basis of "real face" alone.
    60	- **Seedance continuity**: If a Seedance Fast preview works well, production will often stay on Seedance. Tighter framing alone is not a strong reason to move to Kling.
    61	- **Closeup guidance**: Hand closeups, face closeups, beauty shots, and tight crops fit Seedance across all visual styles. Kling is more suitable when readable text/UI or micro-detail accuracy becomes central.
    62	- **15s planning bias**: For character videos with reference images and total target duration ≤15s, a single Seedance production call is often the best starting point.
    63	- **Kling rationale**: If the handoff selects Kling, note a concrete reason such as `copyrighted IP`, `readable text/UI`, or `micro-detail edge case`. Avoid vague reasons like `closeup`, `real face`, `higher precision`, or `better quality`.
    64	- **User intent overrides**: User explicitly wants speed → Grok. User explicitly wants quality → Seedance.
    65	- **Model affects sequence planning**: Seedance allows ≤15s per sequence; Kling allows ≤10s; Grok allows ≤10s. The model choice must be locked before sequence breakdown begins.
    66	
    67	### Pre-call Model Verification (binding for generation-skill)
    68	
    69	The routing decision must specify all four fields consistently:
    70	
    71	| Routing decision | `provider` | `model` | `mode` | `provider_param` key |
    72	|-----------------|-----------|---------|--------|---------------------|
    73	| Seedance | `seedance` | `doubao-seedance-2-0-260128` | `reference2video` | `seedance_reference2video` |
    74	| Seedance Fast | `seedance` | `doubao-seedance-2-0-fast-260128` | `reference2video` | `seedance_reference2video` |
    75	| Kling | `kling` | `kling-v3-omni` | `reference2video` | `kling_reference2video` |
    76	| Grok | `fal` | `xai/grok-imagine-video/text-to-video` | `text2video` | `fal_grok_text2video` |
    77	
    78	## Step 2 — Sequence Planning
    79	
    80	Break the creative script into executable sequences. Each sequence is a self-contained narrative unit that maps to one generation call.
    81	
    82	1. **Set sequence boundaries** — duration per sequence depends on the model: Seedance ≤15s, Kling ≤10s, Grok ≤10s. Let the narrative arc's natural break points drive where sequences split, not a fixed formula.
    83	
    84	2. **Single-call preference**: When total video duration fits within one call (Seedance ≤15s, Kling ≤10s), prefer a single sequence — use prompt-internal timing cues (`[0–5s]`, `[5–10s]`, etc.) to structure beats within a single prompt. Splitting is allowed when there is a clear creative reason (independent scenes, different reference sets, isolated retry needs), but unnecessary splitting wastes API calls, introduces cross-segment consistency risk, and forces post-assembly overhead. For multi-sequence videos, leverage Seedance's 15s cap to reduce sequence count.
    85	
    86	3. **Plan production strategy**:
    87	   - **Parallel** (default) — all sequences generated independently from shared references. Faster, every sequence gets audio co-generation.
    88	   - **Serial** — only when sequences depict continuous unbroken action in the same scene requiring frame-level visual continuity.
    89	   - **Hybrid** — serial for continuous-action blocks, parallel for the rest.
    90	   - **Continuous-action override**: When adjacent sequences depict a **single character's unbroken motion arc** — no scene change, no time skip — those sequences **must be recommended as serial**, regardless of reference coverage. Independent scenes can be parallel.
    91	   - Audio is **not** a factor — Seedance supports audio co-generation in serial continuation.
    92	   - Decide before any generation. State the choice and reasoning.
    93	
    94	## Step 3 — Per-Sequence Visual Design
    95	
    96	For each sequence, design the visual execution. Consult `references/shot-design-principles.md` for shot sequence logic, camera movement narrative functions, and shot connection patterns.
    97	
    98	1. **Shot outline** — core shots within the sequence: what happens, shot type (wide/medium/close/detail), camera movement (static/push-in/pan/orbit/tracking), and the narrative purpose of each shot.
    99	
   100	2. **Timing allocation** — budget time per shot using prompt-internal timing cues (`[0–5s]`, `[5–10s]`). Let the emotional weight drive duration: high-intensity beats get more time, breathing beats get less. For shots with voiceover or readable text, budget ≥ spoken/read duration + 0.5–1s slack (~3–4 Chinese chars/sec, ~2.5–3 English words/sec). See `references/editing-rhythm-design.md` for rhythm curve design and how emotion/information density maps to shot language.
   101	
   102	3. **Emotion & information density arc**: Annotate where each sequence sits on the emotional intensity and information density curves — whether a given moment is a quiet breathing beat, an information-heavy walkthrough, an emotional peak, etc. These two dimensions inform shot language choices (see `references/editing-rhythm-design.md` Section 4).
   103	
   104	4. **Visual Style Convention** (multi-sequence only) — define 4 constants that every sequence shares:
   105	   - **Rendering style** (photorealistic / cinematic / painterly / illustration / motion graphics)
   106	   - **Color palette** (warm/cool/neutral, dominant tones)
   107	   - **Lighting mood** (natural / studio / dramatic / soft / golden hour)
   108	   - **Texture quality** (organic film grain / clean digital / dreamy soft focus)
   109	
   110	   These constants become a **style preamble** that generation-skill prepends to every segment's prompt, preventing each segment from independently interpreting the visual style. When the creative story involves a deliberate style shift (e.g., reality → fantasy), annotate which segments deviate and why. If all segments share one visual world (the common case), state the convention once and note "applies to all sequences."
   111	
   112	5. **Subject consistency plan** — for every subject element (character, product, environment, brand element) appearing in more than one sequence, specify which reference image anchors it and confirm the reference will be passed to every relevant sequence. Detailed reference mapping and gap analysis happens in subject-asset-skill.
   113	
   114	## Step 4 — Audio Design
   115	
   116	Design the complete audio landscape. Audio co-generation is the #1 priority — SFX and on-screen dialogue are always co-generated, never post-produced. See `../generation-skill/references/audio-strategy.md` for the full audio strategy by model and reference combination.
   117	
   118	Before assigning any voice path, read the request through three lenses: **visible presenter speech**, **voiceover**, **mixed**. If the boundary is ambiguous, ask one focused question before planning.
   119	
   120	For each sequence:
   121	
   122	1. **SFX & atmosphere** — always co-generated. Design at least one concrete sound event tied to on-screen action per sequence. Plan a shared ambient foundation across sequences for cross-segment coherence. See `references/audio-design-guide.md` Sections 1–3 for sound-style positioning and audio-visual relationships.
   123	
   124	2. **SFX coherence plan** (multi-sequence only): Define a **shared ambient foundation** — a consistent base-layer SFX character that should persist across all segments (e.g., "warm organic room tone" or "soft outdoor nature ambience"). On top of this base, plan a **designed SFX arc** across segments — how the sound evolves through the narrative, not random per-segment choices. This plan feeds into generation-skill's per-segment SFX prompt writing.
   125	
   126	3. **Dialogue & monologue** (on-screen speech) — always co-generated when the character's speech starts and ends within the sequence. Design the spoken content, emotional register, and rhythm. See `references/dialogue-monologue-design-kb.md` Sections 1–2 for dialogue construction principles.
   127	
   128	4. **Voiceover & narration** (off-screen speech) — post-assembly via TTS for cross-sequence voice consistency. Plan the VO content, pacing, and tone per sequence. Select voice ID from the catalog. See `references/vo-design-principles.md` Sections 1–2 for VO content design and Section 5 for the voice catalog.
   129	
   130	5. **Reference-audio presenter path** — if supplied speech audio or a voice reference is meant to drive visible performance, start with audio-conditioned generation. Seedance often fits best: pass speech audio in `audio_list` with the presenter reference, and split long audio by sequence so each sequence gets only its own ≤15s chunk. If the voice should stay off-screen, keep it in the narration path above.
   131	
   132	6. **BGM timing mode** — determine temporal relationship between music and visuals:
   133	   - **Video-first** (default >15s): generate video first, analyze rhythm, compose BGM to match.
   134	   - **Music-first**: when the concept is rhythm-driven or user provided a music track.
   135	   - **Independent**: when BGM is purely ambient.
   136	   
   137	   Base the recommendation on the creative story's audio-visual relationship. See `../assembly-skill/references/assembly-rules.md` for BGM timing strategy and mix patterns.
   138	
   139	7. **Voice source exclusivity** — each spoken line has exactly one audio source (co-gen OR TTS, never both). Declare the assignment per line before any generation call.
   140	
   141	8. **Audio strategy by model/reference combination** (key decisions):
   142	   - **Seedance with any references** → audio co-gen enabled (`sound: "on"`), design SFX + dialogue in prompt.
   143	   - **Visible presenter + supplied speech/timbre reference** → usually prefer Seedance with per-sequence speech chunks in `audio_list`; this often preserves audio-visual coupling better than later narration.
   144	   - **Kling with reference video** → always silent, extract Sound Reference Context for post BGM/narration.
   145	   - **Single ≤15s Seedance / ≤10s Kling** → design dialogue/monologue + SFX in prompt; allow music + narration in-prompt only when this is the final single delivery.
   146	   - **Multi-sequence** → design SFX + in-sequence dialogue per sequence; keep BGM and off-screen narration for post-assembly.
   147	
   148	## Step 5 — Visual Packaging & Text Design
   149	
   150	Design all on-screen text, brand elements, UI, and decorative graphics. See `references/packaging-keyframe-guide.md` for the complete Layer 1/Layer 2 classification and composition rules, and `references/visual-packaging-guide.md` for overlay types and design patterns.
   151	
   152	### Two-Layer System
   153	
   154	**Layer 1 — AI Generation Layer (via key frame reference images)**: For packaging elements and environment-layer text carriers where visual atmosphere and scene integration matter more than pixel-perfect text accuracy.
   155	
   156	- **What belongs here**: Brand name/logo compositions, decorative typography / artistic text, visual style overlays, product packaging layouts, end cards, sticker-style elements, animated graphic overlays, plus **any in-scene material carrying readable text** (posters, signs, certificates, LED walls, dashboards, app screens, device interfaces, product labels, title cards, picture-in-picture-style screens).
   157	- **How it works**: Generate text-bearing key frame images using image generation tools. Pass to generation via `image_list`. In the prompt, describe how the image's content appears in the video — not the literal text.
   158	- **Readable text rule**: If the audience is supposed to read exact words on screen, those exact glyphs must live in a reference image. Do **not** plan to have the model draw the words from prompt text.
   159	- **Prompt prohibition**: Do not write instructions like `"the screen shows '90%'"` or `"the words XXX appear"` as if the video model should render characters from scratch.
   160	- **In-frame packaging**: Text that is visually integrated into the shot (poster-style headlines, spatially attached text, tracked typography) counts as Layer 1 key frame references.
   161	- **Product UI in scene**: Product screenshots, dashboards, app screens → environment-layer references, not post overlays.
   162	- **Text occlusion planning**: When text/UI may be partially covered, annotate the safer strategy (keep unobstructed, or provide dual-image reference).
   163	
   164	**Layer 2 — Post-Production Layer**: For precise readable timeline text composited after video generation.
   165	
   166	- **Supported when simple and safe-area bounded**: subtitles/captions, short titles, simple CTA lines, and low-density callouts can be planned as post-production text, provided the handoff states their role, approximate timing, platform safe-area tier, and whether they should be plain subtitle treatment or designed text.
   167	- **Still not generally supported as freeform dense overlay planning**: data-heavy information bars, prices, phone numbers, website links, complex multi-line claims, animated PiP/UI panels, or dense dashboard overlays should remain unsupported unless a concrete downstream edit path is explicitly confirmed.
   168	- **Creative script annotation**: use notes like `post_text: short CTA — supported if safe-area checked` or `packaging: data bar — NOT SUPPORTED without confirmed post overlay path`.
   169	
   170	### Classification Decision Tree
   171	
   172	- Brand name / logo → **Layer 1**
   173	- Artistic typography → **Layer 1**
   174	- Decorative stickers / graphic overlays → **Layer 1**
   175	- End card with brand identity → **Layer 1**
   176	- Product UI / dashboard / app screen in scene → **Layer 1** as `ui_ref`
   177	- Poster / sign / certificate / screen / label with readable text in scene → **Layer 1** as environment-layer key frame
   178	- Spatially integrated selling-point text in shot → **Layer 1** as in-frame packaging key frame
   179	- Short selling point text (must be readable) → **Layer 2** only when low-density and safe-area checked; otherwise **Layer 1** key frame or unsupported
   180	- Subtitles / captions → **Layer 2** with explicit **platform safe-area** planning
   181	- Data callouts / numbers → **Not supported** by default (offer Layer 1 as best-effort where appropriate)
   182	- Price / phone number / website link → **Not supported**
   183	
   184	## Step 6 — Pacing Plan
   185	
   186	Design how the overall rhythm flows across sequences (e.g., slow build → climax → resolution). Think about the emotional arc and information density across the full story before diving into individual shots — let the story's rhythm guide shot language choices rather than defaulting to aesthetic habit.
   187	
   188	## Step 7 — Production Handoff
   189	
   190	Package everything for subject-asset-skill and generation-skill as internal context (not shown to user):
   191	
   192	- **Sequence list** — ordered sequences with per-sequence: shot outline, timing, visual design, audio design, packaging elements, reference image assignments.
   193	- **Production model** — which model, with concrete `provider` + `model` + `mode` + `provider_param` key.
   194	- **Production strategy** — parallel / serial / hybrid, with reasoning.
   195	- **Visual Style Convention** — the 4 constants for multi-sequence consistency.
   196	- **Audio plan** — per-sequence SFX/dialogue assignment, VO plan, BGM timing mode, SFX coherence plan, voice source declarations.
   197	- **Packaging plan** — Layer 1 key frame image annotations, plus Layer 2 post-text items with role, approximate timing, platform safe-area tier, and density/feasibility notes.
   198	- **Packaging feasibility discipline** — when writing shot design and packaging notes, avoid concepts that rely on unsupported special characters, decorative Unicode, emoji typography, or mixed-font composition within one text line/track. Script-level packaging should already be production-aware, so downstream skills receive buildable design intent rather than impossible typography wishes.
   199	- **Text motion intent** — for subtitles, titles, and CTA text: define the role (informational caption vs visual packaging), motion intensity (none / restrained / expressive), and why it matches the video's tone. Do not specify implementation details such as ASS tags, keyframe arrays, coordinates, or exact animation durations.
   200	- **Pacing plan** — rhythm flow across sequences.

content.text:
**Production Plan (internal):**
- **Model**: Seedance (`doubao-seedance-2-0-260128`), parallel
- **Visual Style Convention**: Cinematic-documentary look, cool-to-neutral palette with accent orange/red, soft professional lighting, clean digital texture
- **Voice**: TTS (off-screen narrator), voice `George` — authoritative male
- **BGM**: Independent (upbeat corporate tech)
- **Audio strategy**: co-gen SFX in each video segment, TTS VO in post, no BGM in generation calls

**Sequences:**
1. Hook (15s) — fast montage of frustrated users
2. Sign 1 (15s) — outdated vs modern website side-by-side
3. Sign 2 (15s) — phone POV struggling with mobile site
4. Sign 3 (15s) — loading spinner, person closes laptop
5. Sign 4 (15s) — messy vs clean website dashboard
6. Sign 5 (15s) — analytics graph high traffic, zero conversions
7. Solution (15s) — person checking phone/tablet/laptop with green checkmarks
8. CTA (10s) — ebook mockup with download button arrow

Firing all VO, BGM, and video generation simultaneously:

elevenlabs_text2speech.text: [excited] Is your website secretly costing you customers? [shouts] Here are 5 signs it's time for an upgrade — and you need to hear this NOW.

Sign number one — outdated design. If your website looks like it was built ten years ago, visitors won't trust your business. Period. First impressions happen in under three seconds. A cluttered, old-fashioned design tells people you're behind the times — and they leave. Look at this. An old clunky site full of tiny text and dated graphics — versus a clean, modern flat design that instantly says "professional." That snap judgment decides whether they stay or bounce. And trust me — they're bouncing.

Sign number two — not mobile friendly. Over sixty percent of web traffic now comes from phones. If your site forces people to pinch, zoom, and scroll sideways just to read your menu — they're gone. Watch this. Tiny text. Buttons you can't tap. Layouts breaking on every screen. Now look at a properly responsive site — it just works. Smooth. Clean. Easy. If your site doesn't work on a phone, you're handing customers straight to your competitors.

Sign number three — slow speed. Every second your site takes to load, you're losing customers. Studies show that 53% of users abandon a site that takes more than three seconds to load. Three seconds! Watch that timer count down — one, two, three — and that spinner just keeps spinning. Your visitor's patience runs out, the laptop slams shut, and they're off to Google finding someone else. Speed is not a technical problem. It's a revenue problem.

Sign number four — hard to update. If every time you want to change a price, add a service, or post a blog — you feel like you need a computer science degree — that is a problem. When your website is hard to update, you stop updating it. Your content goes stale. Google notices. Your rankings drop. Look at this — a messy, overwhelming dashboard that nobody wants to touch. Now compare that to a clean drag-and-drop builder where anyone can make changes in minutes. Your website should work for you — not against you.

Sign number five — and this is the big one — no conversions. You might be getting traffic. Hundreds of visitors a month. But if nobody's filling out your form, calling your number, or buying your product — your website is failing at its one job. Look at this graph. Traffic going up, up, up — but sales? Flat. Zero. A big red zero staring right at you. Your website isn't a digital brochure. It's your best salesperson. And right now — it's not selling.

Here's the good news. A great website doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. It just has to be fast, mobile-friendly, modern, and built to convert. You don't need to be a tech wizard to fix this. Whether you're checking it on your phone, your tablet, or your laptop — a well-built site should look great and work perfectly on all of them. And every page should have one clear goal — get the visitor to take action. That's it. Fast. Responsive. Modern. Converts. Fix those five things and your website becomes your most powerful business tool.

Stop losing customers today. Download my free guide below — it walks you step by step through exactly how to fix your website and start getting more clients. The link is right below this video. Go grab it now.

elevenlabs_text2speech.text: Sign number one — outdated design. If your website looks like it was built ten years ago, visitors won't trust your business. Period. First impressions happen in under three seconds. A cluttered, old-fashioned design tells people you're behind the times — and they leave. An old clunky site full of tiny text and dated graphics versus a clean, modern flat design that instantly says professional. That snap judgment decides whether they stay or bounce. And trust me — they're bouncing.

Sign number two — not mobile friendly. Over sixty percent of web traffic now comes from phones. If your site forces people to pinch, zoom, and scroll sideways just to read your menu — they're gone. Tiny text. Buttons you can't tap. Layouts breaking on every screen. Now compare that to a properly responsive site — smooth, clean, easy. If your site doesn't work on a phone, you're handing customers straight to your competitors.

Sign number three — slow speed. Every second your site takes to load, you are losing customers. Studies show that 53 percent of users abandon a site that takes more than three seconds to load. Three seconds! Watch that timer count down — one, two, three — and that spinner just keeps spinning. Your visitor's patience runs out, the laptop slams shut, and they're off to Google finding someone else. Speed is not a technical problem. It's a revenue problem.

Sign number four — hard to update. If every time you want to change a price, add a service, or post a blog — you feel like you need a computer science degree — that is a problem. When your website is hard to update, you stop updating it. Your content goes stale. Google notices. Your rankings drop. A messy, overwhelming dashboard that nobody wants to touch — versus a clean drag-and-drop builder where anyone can make changes in minutes. Your website should work for you — not against you.

seedance_reference2video.prompt: Cinematic documentary look, cool neutral palette with deep blues and sharp whites, soft professional studio lighting, clean digital texture. Fast montage of frustrated office workers clicking frantically on slow-loading websites, one person aggressively closing a laptop lid, another shaking their head staring at a spinning loading circle on a desktop monitor, a young woman turning away from her computer screen with a disappointed expression, quick cuts every 1-2 seconds building urgency and tension. Sound effects: rapid mouse clicking, keyboard tapping, frustrated exhale, laptop closing thud.

Cinematic documentary look, cool blue-grey palette with red accent highlights, dramatic professional lighting, clean digital texture. A large digital countdown timer on screen counting from 5 down to 1 with red digits, beside it a browser tab showing a spinning loading circle that never resolves. Cut to close-up of a person's face, impatient, checking watch, tapping fingers on desk. Then the person reaches forward and forcefully slams the laptop shut in frustration. Final shot: dark closed laptop on a desk. Sound effects: ticking clock countdown, spinning wheel hum, loud satisfying laptop slam, silence.

Cinematic documentary look, cool neutral palette with blue and green accents, soft office lighting, clean digital texture. Split-screen: left side shows a confused business owner in front of an overwhelming chaotic website dashboard with dozens of confusing menu options, cryptic buttons everywhere, the person squinting and looking overwhelmed, head in hands. Right side shows the same person in front of a clean drag-and-drop website builder interface with large clear sections, simple controls, the person smiling and confidently dragging content blocks into place with ease. Sound effects: confused keyboard mashing on left, smooth satisfying drag-and-drop click on right.

Cinematic documentary look, cool dark palette with red and orange accent highlights, dramatic studio lighting, clean digital texture. Close-up of an analytics dashboard on a large monitor, an animated line graph showing website traffic climbing steadily upward in blue, while a second line for sales and conversions stays completely flat near the bottom in red. A bold red number zero pulses and glows on screen. Cut to a business owner staring at the screen with arms crossed, expression of concern and disbelief. Camera slowly zooms into the zero on screen. Sound effects: data dashboard notification beeps, tense low hum, dramatic stinger.

Cinematic documentary look, warm professional palette with clean whites and vibrant green accents, soft bright natural lighting, clean digital texture. Uplifting montage: a professional woman sitting at a bright modern desk checking her phone showing a sleek responsive website with a glowing green checkmark animation appearing over it, then picking up a tablet and swiping through a fast beautiful website with another green checkmark, then leaning forward to her laptop with a modern website open and third green checkmark animating in. She leans back smiling confidently. Final wide shot: all three devices on the desk showing the same beautiful responsive website. Sound effects: three satisfying positive chime sounds with each checkmark, uplifting ambient whoosh.

Cinematic documentary look, warm clean palette with white background and bold orange accents, bright soft professional studio lighting, clean digital texture. A professional ebook mockup cover slides into frame from the right, clean white and orange design with bold readable title. Two large bold animated arrows pulse and point downward toward a glowing orange download button below the ebook. Camera slowly zooms into the ebook cover as the arrows continue to pulse rhythmically. Small sparkle effects appear around the download button. Sound effects: smooth slide-in whoosh, pulsing button glow hum, upbeat notification chime.
